title: OOM from Implicit Runtime Layout Transposes
scenario: An edge gateway (4GB unified RAM) runs a 200MB FP16 Transformer model via an edge inference runtime. Theoretical peak activation memory for a 1024-token sequence is 120MB. Yet, inference crashes with an OOM error when processing 1024 tokens. Profiling reveals peak memory usage spikes to 3.5GB during the self-attention layers.
common_mistake: Assuming a memory leak in the application code or blaming the OS for fragmentation, leading to host-level memory tuning or reducing batch size to 1.
realistic_solution: The failure stems from the interaction between dynamic tensor shapes, the runtime's memory arena, and implicit layout conversions. The model was exported from PyTorch in NCHW layout, but the edge runtime's optimized matrix multiplication kernels (e.g., using specific DSP intrinsics or Tensor Cores) strictly require NHWC or blocked layouts. Because the model uses dynamic sequence lengths, the runtime compiler cannot perform a static end-to-end layout optimization pass. Instead, it dynamically injects explicit `Transpose` nodes before and after *every* MatMul in the attention blocks. The framewo
napkin_math: Attention matrix at 1024 tokens, 12 heads: 1024 * 1024 * 12 * 2 bytes (FP16) = ~25MB. Implicit transposes create 4 overlapping copies per tensor (NCHW->NHWC, NHWC->NCHW) = 100MB per attention block. Across 24 layers, dynamic memory planner fails to reuse buffers: 100MB * 24 layers = 2.4GB. Adding Q, K, V tensors + memory fragmentation pushes peak memory to ~3.5GB. Static planning + NHWC conversion bounds peak memory to ~120MB.

title: Continuous Roofline Profiling for Multimodal
scenario: Your team is building a multimodal serving cluster using 40GB A100 GPUs (1.5TB/s memory bandwidth, 312 TFLOPS bfloat16). The system handles concurrent text generation (auto-regressive decoding) and image generation (diffusion steps). At peak load of 5,000 QPS, tail latency spikes from 150ms to over 2 seconds. You need to architect a continuous profiling and telemetry framework to dynamically detect hardware bottlenecks and inform the global scheduler. How do you design this profiling system to decompose the performance limiters?
common_mistake: Relying on SM utilization (e.g., nvidia-smi volatile GPU util) which masks the difference between being stalled on memory bandwidth versus actively computing.
realistic_solution: Architect a continuous profiling agent using hardware performance counters (DCGM/CUPTI) to sample SM active cycles, Tensor Core utilization, and DRAM read/write bytes at 10ms intervals. The system must dynamically compute the Arithmetic Intensity (AI) in real-time to build a live Roofline model. Text decoding has an AI of ~2 FLOPs/byte (heavily memory-bandwidth bound), while batched diffusion steps have an AI of >300 FLOPs/byte (compute bound). By emitting these metrics to a time-series database, the profiler allows the scheduler to perform bottleneck decomposition. The architectural outcome i
napkin_math: A100 Ridge point = 312e12 FLOPs / 1.5e12 Bytes/s = 208 FLOPs/byte. Text decoding (batch size 1): requires loading 2 bytes per parameter for 2 FLOPs of compute -> AI = 1 FLOP/byte. Max throughput = 1.5TB/s * 1 FLOP/byte = 1.5 TFLOPS (0.48% compute utilization). Image diffusion (batch size 4): AI ~500 FLOPs/byte. Max throughput = 312 TFLOPS (compute bound). Co-location yields ~98% memory bus utilization and ~95% SM utilization.

title: E2E Edge Benchmarking Under Thermal Limits
scenario: You are deploying a cascaded speech translation pipeline (ASR -> MT -> TTS) on an edge SoC with a strict 5W Sustained Power limit and 1.5GB RAM. Component micro-benchmarks show latencies of ASR: 200ms, MT: 150ms, TTS: 250ms. However, during an MLPerf-style end-to-end sustained offline benchmark, P99 latency degrades to 2.8 seconds after 30 seconds of load. Architect an E2E benchmarking and profiling harness to isolate and decompose this regression.
common_mistake: Summing isolated micro-benchmark latencies to estimate E2E performance, completely ignoring thermal throttling, DVFS state changes, and memory bus contention.
realistic_solution: Design an E2E macro-benchmarking harness that runs continuous, pipelined queries rather than isolated inferences. The architecture must integrate three telemetry streams: 1) Application-level trace spans for ASR, MT, and TTS execution times. 2) OS-level memory allocation hooks to track RAM fragmentation and swap. 3) Hardware-level metrics reading sysfs thermal zones and INA219 power rail sensors at 100Hz. The bottleneck analysis will reveal that pipelining the models causes the SoC to draw 8W, exceeding the 5W thermal envelope. After 30 seconds, the SoC hits 90C and the OS drops the NPU/CPU DV
napkin_math: Micro-benchmark sum: 200 + 150 + 250 = 600ms. Power usage: ASR (3W) + MT (4W) + TTS (3W). Pipelined peak power = 10W (exceeds 5W cap). At 10W, SoC thermal capacity is exhausted in 30s. DVFS throttles clock speed by 60% to reduce power to 4W. New latencies at 40% clock speed: 600ms / 0.4 = 1500ms. If memory swapping occurs due to 1.5GB limit (e.g., 500MB swap at 1GB/s), add 500ms -> Total E2E latency = 2000ms to 2.8s under contention.

title: Auto-Tuning Budget vs Dynamic Batching
scenario: Deploying a dense retrieval model (100 unique MatMul shapes) on L4 GPUs. Auto-tuning a single batch size takes 20 minutes. We need to support 64 dynamic batch sizes (1 to 64). Full auto-tuning takes 64 * 20 = 1280 mins (21 hours). Our CI/CD enforces a 3-hour (180 mins) max compilation budget. Eager mode yields 10ms latency (failing 5ms SLA). How do you meet the SLA and budget?
common_mistake: Falling back to eager mode or heuristic compilation for all batch sizes, missing the SLA.
realistic_solution: Implement sequence bucketization in the compiler frontend. Group the 64 batch sizes into 8 buckets (e.g., 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 48, 64). Configure the auto-tuner (like TVM Ansor or XLA) to only search and compile optimized kernels for these 8 specific shapes. At runtime, pad incoming requests to the nearest bucket size. The compute overhead from padding is completely eclipsed by the speedup of running heavily optimized, auto-tuned kernels, while fitting comfortably within the CI/CD pipeline's time constraints.
napkin_math:
Full Tuning: 64 sizes * 20 min = 1,280 mins (> 180m budget).
Bucket Tuning (8 buckets): 8 * 20 min = 160 mins (< 180m budget).
Latency: Tuned exact-match latency is ~3ms. Worst-case padding adds ~33% compute overhead. 3ms * 1.33 = ~4ms average latency. 4ms < 5ms SLA.

title: Evaluating MLIR Fusion for Bandwidth
scenario: Optimizing a custom Transformer on A100 GPUs (1.5 TB/s effective HBM bandwidth). Profiling reveals a bottleneck in a custom gating network: Out = Tanh(X) * Sigmoid(Y). X and Y are large activations, each 400M FP16 elements (800MB each). The current ML compiler lowers this to 3 sequential kernels (Tanh, Sigmoid, Multiply), taking ~3.7ms total. You propose an MLIR lowering pass to fuse these element-wise operations. What is the expected speedup?
common_mistake: Assuming compute is the bottleneck and attempting to optimize the FP16 math instructions.
realistic_solution: The bottleneck is not compute, but memory bandwidth due to materializing intermediate tensors to HBM. By introducing a custom fusion pass at the MLIR linalg/tensor level, the compiler can lower the entire block into a single GPU kernel. This fused kernel loads X and Y directly into SRAM/registers, performs the Tanh, Sigmoid, and Multiply operations on-chip, and writes Out back to HBM. This eliminates the reading and writing of the intermediate Tanh(X) and Sigmoid(Y) tensors, drastically reducing total memory traffic and directly cutting latency.
napkin_math:
Unfused Traffic:
1) Tanh: Read X (800MB), Write T1 (800MB) = 1.6GB
2) Sigmoid: Read Y (800MB), Write T2 (800MB) = 1.6GB
3) Multiply: Read T1 (800MB), Read T2 (800MB), Write Out (800MB) = 2.4GB
Total Unfused = 5.6GB. Time = 5.6GB / 1.5 TB/s = ~3.73ms.
Fused Traffic:
Read X (800MB), Read Y (800MB), Write Out (800MB) = 2.4GB
Total Fused = 2.4GB. Time = 2.4GB / 1.5 TB/s = 1.6ms.
Expected Latency Reduction = 3.73ms - 1.6ms = 2.13ms (a 57% speedup).

title: The CPU Overhead Anomaly
scenario: You deploy a 7B LLM on a single A100 (80GB) using a serving framework configured with CUDA graphs to minimize latency. Production traffic consists of heavily mixed sequence lengths (input lengths vary continuously between 32 and 1024 tokens). Profiling reveals that average token generation latency has unexpectedly spiked from 45ms to 120ms, CPU utilization on the inference worker is pinned at 100%, and GPU SM utilization has plummeted to 25%.
common_mistake: Assuming the GPU is compute-bound by the varying sequence lengths and attempting to optimize the attention kernel, or adding more CPU threads.
realistic_solution: The symptom (100% CPU, low GPU utilization, high latency) points directly to graph recompilation and framework dispatch overhead. CUDA graphs require fixed memory addresses and grid dimensions. When input tensor shapes change dynamically (e.g., varying sequence lengths), the graph capture mechanism cannot replay the existing graph. Instead, the framework either falls back to eager mode dispatch (incurring high CPU overhead per operation) or, worse, triggers a full recompilation of the computation graph for the new shape. The solution requires hardware-compiler-algorithm co-design: Algorithmica
napkin_math: Eager dispatch overhead per layer is ~15µs. For a 32-layer model with 6 ops per layer = 192 ops. Dispatch overhead = 192 * 15µs = 2.88ms per token. CUDA graph replay overhead is ~10µs total. However, graph recompilation costs ~200ms. If 50% of the varying requests trigger a recompilation: (0.5 * 200ms) + (0.5 * 10µs) = ~100ms of average overhead added per request. Bucketing to 4 fixed sizes reduces runtime recompilations to 0, ensuring the 10µs replay path is always taken.

title: The Memory-Bound Custom MLP
scenario: You are optimizing a custom 13B parameter model on an H100 GPU (3.35 TB/s HBM bandwidth). The model uses a novel activation function in its MLP: `CustomGLU(x) = x * tanh(alpha * x + beta)`. For a batch size of 4 and sequence length of 2048, the MLP expansion layer (hidden size 5120 -> 13824) is a major bottleneck. Profiling shows SM compute utilization is only 15%, but HBM bandwidth utilization is pinned at 96% (~3.2 TB/s).
common_mistake: Attempting to quantize the weights (e.g., to INT8 or FP8) to reduce memory bandwidth, missing that the bottleneck is the intermediate activation reads/writes.
realistic_solution: The symptom (low SM utilization, maxed HBM bandwidth on element-wise operations) indicates that intermediate tensors are being fully materialized to global memory (HBM) instead of being kept in SRAM/registers. In standard eager execution, the framework dispatches separate kernels for `alpha * x`, `+ beta`, `tanh`, and `* x`. Each discrete kernel must read from and write back to HBM. To fix this, you must apply operator fusion (via `torch.compile`, XLA, or writing a custom Triton kernel). Fusion combines these sequential element-wise operations into a single kernel. The intermediate results (`a
napkin_math: Expansion tensor `x` size: 4 (batch) * 2048 (seq) * 13824 (dim) * 2 bytes (BF16) = 226 MB. Eager execution memory traffic: 1) `a = alpha * x` (Read 226MB, Write 226MB). 2) `b = a + beta` (Read 226MB, Write 226MB). 3) `c = tanh(b)` (Read 226MB, Write 226MB). 4) `d = x * c` (Read 226MB of x + 226MB of c, Write 226MB). Total HBM traffic = 9 * 226 MB = 2.03 GB. At 3.35 TB/s, memory movement takes ~0.6ms. Fused kernel: Read `x` (226MB), compute all math in registers, Write `d` (226MB). Total traffic = 452 MB. Time = 452 MB / 3.35 TB/s = ~0.13ms. Fusion yields a ~4.6x speedup on this block.

title: The Phantom Memory Pool
scenario: A production recommendation model runs efficiently in PyTorch eager mode, using 60GB of an 80GB A100 during the forward pass. To increase QPS, you enable CUDA graphs. You run a standard eager warmup pass, then trigger the graph capture. Immediately, the system throws a CUDA Out of Memory (OOM) error, even though the batch size and model architecture haven't changed. Model weights are 20GB, and peak activations are 40GB.
common_mistake: Assuming graph metadata takes up 20GB+ of VRAM, and arbitrarily reducing the batch size to compensate, which destroys the throughput gains you wanted.
realistic_solution: The symptom (OOM occurring exactly at graph capture time despite eager mode fitting comfortably) is caused by how graph capture mechanisms handle memory planning. CUDA graphs require stable virtual memory addresses to guarantee safe replay. Because of this, the framework allocates a dedicated, contiguous memory pool specifically for the graph's intermediates. When you run the eager warmup pass, the framework's caching allocator reserves 40GB for the activations. Unless explicitly cleared, this 40GB remains in the reserved pool. When graph capture starts, it requests a *new* 40GB memory pool fo
napkin_math: Total VRAM: 80GB. Weights footprint: 20GB. Forward pass activations: 40GB. Eager memory state after warmup: 20GB (Weights) + 40GB (Eager caching allocator reserved) = 60GB. Graph capture initiates and requests a private memory pool for its activations: +40GB. Total memory requested = 60GB + 40GB = 100GB. 100GB > 80GB, resulting in OOM. By emptying the cache prior to capture, the eager allocator drops to 0GB reserved, meaning the capture only requires 20GB (W) + 40GB (Graph Pool) = 60GB, safely fitting in the 80GB limit.

title: Tiered KV-Cache Page Size Tradeoffs
scenario: You are architecting a tiered KV-cache (HBM3 + Host DRAM) for serving a 70B LLM (256 KB KV-cache per token) handling highly variable sequence lengths (100 to 50,000 tokens). To prevent OOMs, the system aggressively swaps KV-cache to Host DRAM via PCIe Gen5 x16 (64 GB/s). You must determine the uniform memory block size for the PagedAttention memory manager. Option A uses 16-token blocks (4MB); Option B uses 256-token blocks (64MB). Small blocks minimize internal fragmentation but risk poor PCIe utilization due to scatter-gather DMA overhead during Host-to-Device prefetching. Large blocks optim
common_mistake: Selecting 256-token blocks to maximize PCIe throughput, ignoring that internal fragmentation will waste ~32GB of HBM across a typical batch, causing OOMs before PCIe bandwidth becomes the bottleneck.
realistic_solution: Adopt a decoupled, two-level memory management architecture to get the best of both worlds. Use 16-token (4MB) logical pages in HBM to strictly minimize internal fragmentation, which is critical since HBM is the primary capacity bottleneck. For a batch of 1024 requests, large 64MB pages would waste ~32GB of HBM due to internal fragmentation (average 32MB wasted per sequence). Small 4MB pages reduce this waste to ~2GB, freeing 30GB of HBM for larger batch sizes. To resolve the PCIe scatter-gather latency tradeoff, manage Host DRAM in large 256-token (64MB) physical segments. Implement an asynch
napkin_math:
1. Internal Fragmentation (256-token blocks): Average waste = 128 tokens = 32MB/request.
2. Total waste for 1024 requests = 1024 * 32MB = 32.7 GB of HBM.
3. Internal Fragmentation (16-token blocks): Average waste = 8 tokens = 2MB/request.
4. Total waste = 1024 * 2MB = 2.0 GB. HBM Saved = 30.7 GB.
5. PCIe Gen5 x16 bandwidth = 64 GB/s. A 64MB transfer takes exactly 1ms.
6. DMA setup overhead is ~5us per transaction. 16 uncoalesced 4MB transfers add 16 * 5us = 80us overhead (8% penalty) plus severe bus contention. A single coalesced 64MB transfer amortizes this to <0.5% overhead.

title: NPU Fallback via Dynamic Shapes
scenario: A vision model is deployed on an edge NPU (4 TOPS). Inference latency is consistently 12ms for 224x224 inputs. When the camera switches to a 256x256 resolution stream, latency suddenly spikes to 180ms per frame. Profiling shows 100% CPU usage during these spikes.
common_mistake: Assuming the NPU compute scales linearly with pixels (256^2 / 224^2 * 12ms = ~15.6ms) and blaming memory bandwidth for the rest.
realistic_solution: The root cause is a graph capture and framework dispatch failure. The model was compiled using an Ahead-of-Time (AOT) graph capture mode that assumed static shapes (224x224). When the dynamic shape (256x256) arrives, the framework dispatcher fails to match the compiled NPU engine's signature. It catches the shape mismatch and triggers a fallback to unoptimized, eager execution on the edge CPU, abandoning the NPU entirely.
napkin_math: NPU expected latency: (256*256) / (224*224) * 12ms = 15.6ms. Actual CPU fallback latency: ~150ms for eager execution + 18ms for recompilation/dispatch overhead + 12ms for initial NPU setup/failure = 180ms.

title: SRAM Spills in Aggressive Fusion
scenario: You deploy MobileNetV2 on an edge NPU with 2MB of SRAM. A new compiler pass aggressively fuses Depthwise Conv + 1x1 Conv to reduce DRAM trips. Unexpectedly, latency increases from 5ms to 18ms, and DRAM bandwidth usage triples compared to the unfused version.
common_mistake: Believing operator fusion always strictly reduces memory bandwidth by keeping intermediate activations in registers.
realistic_solution: The aggressive fusion creates a monolithic kernel that requires materializing the large intermediate activation tensor (between the Depthwise and 1x1 Convs) entirely in SRAM. Because this intermediate tensor exceeds the 2MB SRAM limit, the memory planner is forced to spill the tensor to DRAM per tile. The unfused version executed sequentially, allowing smaller memory footprints that fit entirely within SRAM without spilling.
napkin_math: Intermediate tensor at early layer: 112x112 spatial x 144 channels x 2 bytes (FP16) = 3,612,672 bytes (~3.44 MB). Since 3.44 MB > 2 MB SRAM, the compiler spills. Spill/Fill adds (3.44MB * 2) / 10GB/s DRAM bandwidth = ~0.68ms per tile. Across 10 tiles, this adds ~6.8ms + high overhead, crippling performance.

title: JIT Tracing Memory Leak
scenario: An NLP model deployed on an edge CPU processes variable-length text sequences. Inference takes 50ms, using 200MB memory. Over 24 hours of processing random lengths, memory grows linearly by 2MB per inference until the system OOMs at 4GB.
common_mistake: Blaming standard heap fragmentation or a Python garbage collection failure for the gradual memory growth.
realistic_solution: The graph capture mode used was JIT tracing, which hardcodes static shapes. The framework dispatcher maintains an LRU cache of compiled execution graphs. Because inputs are variable-length, almost every sequence length is unique, causing continuous cache misses. The framework recompiles a new static graph for each length, and the memory planner allocates new cached workspaces, leaking 2MB per unique length until OOM.
napkin_math: Available memory: 4GB - 200MB base = 3.8GB. 3.8GB / 2MB per compiled graph = 1900 unique sequence lengths before OOM. If 1 unique length arrives per minute, OOM occurs in 1900 mins (~31.6 hours).

title: Emulation Overhead in Fused DSP Ops
scenario: On a mobile SoC (CPU + DSP), an unfused model takes 20ms (15ms Conv2D on DSP, 5ms Swish activation on CPU). You enable graph rewriting to fuse Conv2D + Swish and assign it to the DSP. Latency skyrockets to 45ms.
common_mistake: Assuming the DSP simply has a lower clock speed than the CPU, making all pointwise operations slower.
realistic_solution: The DSP lacks native hardware instructions for the exponential function required by Swish (x * sigmoid(x)). The compiler successfully fused the operations, and the framework dispatcher sent the entire subgraph to the DSP. However, the DSP runtime is forced to emulate exp() using a Taylor series approximation in its scalar ALU, taking hundreds of cycles per element. The hardware-compiler co-design failed to check hardware op support before fusion.
napkin_math: Unfused: 15ms (Conv) + 5ms (CPU Swish) = 20ms. Fused DSP: 15ms (Conv) + (5ms * 6x emulation penalty on DSP scalar ALU) = 45ms.

title: Constant Folding Binary Bloat
scenario: Deploying a model to an MCU with 2MB Flash and 512KB SRAM. Base weights are 1.2MB. You enable a constant folding compiler pass to precompute a static 'Embedding -> Dense' block. The resulting compiled binary jumps to 3.8MB, failing the Flash allocation.
common_mistake: Assuming constant folding always reduces model size by eliminating runtime operations and intermediate variables.
realistic_solution: Constant folding evaluates the subgraph during compilation and materializes the output tensor directly into the graph as a static constant. The intermediate expanded tensor from the Dense layer is significantly larger than the original compressed embedding weights and input indices combined, replacing a small weight matrix with a massive precomputed activation matrix.
napkin_math: Original Embedding: 1000 vocab x 64 dim = 64KB. Input indices: 256. Output of folded Dense layer: 256 seq x 512 dim x 4 bytes (FP32) = 524KB. The folded constant (524KB) + other weights exceeds the 2MB Flash limit when multiple such blocks are folded.

title: Dynamic Control Flow Graph Breakages
scenario: An object detection model on an edge GPU is exported via JIT tracing. It averages 15ms/frame. When viewing complex scenes with >50 detected objects, latency spikes to 85ms, causing frame drops in the autonomous tracking system.
common_mistake: Assuming the GPU's compute capacity is simply overwhelmed by the mathematical load of processing 50+ bounding boxes.
realistic_solution: Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) outputs a dynamic number of bounding boxes. The graph capture mode (tracing) unrolled the loop and forced a static shape (e.g., max 50 boxes). When the dynamic control flow exceeds 50 boxes, a 'graph break' occurs. The framework dispatcher invalidates the optimized GPU engine and falls back to executing eager framework code on the CPU, incurring massive host-device transfer and unoptimized compute overhead.
napkin_math: Normal GPU inference: 15ms. Graph break overhead: 10ms (CPU-GPU memory copy for 50+ boxes) + 60ms (CPU eager execution of remainder of graph) = 70ms penalty. Total: 15ms + 70ms = 85ms.

title: FSDP AllGather PFC Storms at 4K Scale
scenario: You are debugging a massive training stall on a 4,096-GPU cloud cluster (RoCEv2 network, 400Gbps per GPU) training a 70B LLM with FSDP. At 1,024 GPUs, iteration time is a stable 350ms. Scaling to 4,096 GPUs, iteration time fluctuates wildly between 800ms and 3,000ms. GPU compute utilization drops below 35%. Profiling shows FSDP `AllGather` operations taking >600ms on average with massive tail latency. Network telemetry shows 0 dead links, but high rates of PFC (Priority Flow Control) pause frames and subsequent Tx/Rx bandwidth dropping to near zero intermittently on specific spine switches.
common_mistake: Assuming the network is oversubscribed or nodes are failing, and attempting to reduce batch size or simply replace 'straggler' nodes without addressing the routing layer.
realistic_solution: The root cause is ECMP (Equal-Cost Multi-Path) hash collisions causing elephant flow congestion. In RoCEv2, NCCL `AllGather` establishes long-lived, high-bandwidth RDMA queue pairs. Because ECMP routes flows based on a static hash of the 5-tuple (IPs, Ports, Protocol), at 4,096 GPUs, multiple large flows inevitably hash to the same physical spine link. This causes microbursts and buffer overflows, triggering PFC pause frames. These pause frames propagate down to the leaf switches, causing a 'PFC storm' or head-of-line blocking that stalls the ring/tree collectives globally. To fix this: 1) Swi
napkin_math: Switch buffer size = 32MB. If 2 elephant flows of 400Gbps (50GB/s) collide on a single 400Gbps uplink due to ECMP hash collision, the excess arrival rate is 50GB/s. Time to fill a 32MB shared buffer = 32MB / 50GB/s = 0.64ms. Once filled, PFC pause is sent. If NCCL step size is 8MB, transmission takes 8MB / 50GB/s = 160μs. The switch buffer fills and halts traffic before even 4 collective steps complete, causing cascading stalls across the entire 4,096-GPU synchronization group.

title: Optimizing FP8 Formats for LLM Training
scenario: You are migrating a 175B parameter LLM training job on a 1024-GPU H100 cluster from BF16 to FP8 to increase throughput from 140 TFLOPs/GPU to 280 TFLOPs/GPU. You must map the FP8 formats (E4M3 and E5M2) to the forward pass activations, backward pass gradients, and weight updates. Incorrect mapping causes immediate loss divergence.
common_mistake: Using E4M3 for both forward activations and backward gradients to maximize precision.
realistic_solution: In FP8 training, representational requirements differ fundamentally between passes. Activations and weights in the forward pass require high precision to maintain model quality, but have a relatively bounded dynamic range. Thus, E4M3 (4 exponent bits, 3 mantissa bits) is optimal, providing higher precision. Conversely, gradients in the backward pass exhibit a massive dynamic range (often spanning 4-5 orders of magnitude) and are highly susceptible to underflow. Therefore, E5M2 (5 exponent bits, 2 mantissa bits) is required for gradients to prevent underflow to zero. Weights must still be kept
napkin_math: E4M3 max value = 448, min subnormal = 2^-9 (approx 0.0019). E5M2 max = 57344, min subnormal = 2^-16 (approx 1.5e-5). Deep network gradients routinely hit 1e-4, which underflows to exactly 0.0 in E4M3, freezing training. E5M2 safely captures down to 1.5e-5.

title: Attention Softmax FP16 Overflow
scenario: You are serving a 70B model with a 32k context window. The KV cache is stored in FP16 to fit within 4x80GB A100s. At context lengths below 16k, generation is flawless. As soon as the context exceeds 16k tokens, the model outputs completely black/empty tokens (NaNs).
common_mistake: Assuming the KV cache memory allocation is fragmenting and causing out-of-memory errors that fail silently.
realistic_solution: The failure is a numerical range overflow in the attention softmax calculation. In self-attention, the dot product of Query and Key is scaled by 1/sqrt(d_k). For long sequences, accumulating attention scores can result in pre-softmax logits that exceed FP16's maximum representable value. Furthermore, the exponential function in softmax exp(x) will overflow FP16 if x > 11. FlashAttention mitigates this by maintaining a running maximum and scaling on the fly, but if the intermediate accumulators for the softmax denominator aren't explicitly cast to FP32 in the kernel, the sum of thousands of exp
napkin_math: FP16 max value = 65504. If average exp(logit) is just 5.0, summing over 16,000 tokens yields 80,000. 80,000 > 65504, causing an immediate overflow to Infinity. Softmax division (Inf/Inf) yields NaN.

title: INT8 KV Cache Per-Tensor Quantization Failure
scenario: To reduce serving costs, you implement INT8 KV cache quantization on a 13B LLM, cutting KV cache memory from 40GB to 20GB. You use standard per-tensor symmetric quantization. The model's perplexity degrades drastically, and it begins repeating phrases endlessly. The weights remain in FP16.
common_mistake: Assuming INT8 simply lacks the precision for the attention mechanism and moving to a 16-bit format is strictly required.
realistic_solution: The degradation is caused by massive outlier activations in specific hidden channels of the LLM. In large language models, a few feature dimensions consistently exhibit magnitudes up to 100x larger than the rest. With per-tensor symmetric quantization, the quantization scale factor is determined by the absolute maximum value in the entire tensor. The outliers force a very large scale factor, which crushes the normal activations (which are close to zero) into a single INT8 bin (zero). This destroys the representational capacity of the attention keys/values. The architectural fix is to use per-c
napkin_math: Let max outlier = 127.0. INT8 range is [-127, 127]. Scale factor S = 127 / 127 = 1.0. Normal activation value = 0.4. Quantized value = round(0.4 / 1.0) = 0. All normal features are erased, leaving only the outlier channels to drive attention.

title: Mixed Precision Optimizer State Underflow
scenario: You are pre-training a 10B parameter model. To fit the training job onto 8x 40GB A100 GPUs, you aggressively cast the Adam optimizer states (momentum, variance) and the master weights from FP32 to FP16, saving ~80GB of memory. After 5,000 steps, the training loss plateaus completely.
common_mistake: Believing that the learning rate is too high and causing FP16 overflow in the gradients.
realistic_solution: The training has stalled due to weight update underflow, sometimes called 'swamping'. In deep networks, the optimizer weight updates (learning rate * gradient / sqrt(variance)) become extremely small as training progresses. FP16 has only 10 bits of mantissa precision. When adding a tiny update to a relatively large weight, the update falls below the precision gap (machine epsilon) of the weight's current FP16 value, resulting in an effective update of exactly zero. The architecture must maintain the master weights in FP32 so that these tiny high-precision updates can be accumulated over many s
napkin_math: FP16 machine epsilon is 2^-11 (approx 0.000488). If a weight is 1.0, the next representable FP16 number is 1.000488. If the Adam update is 1e-4, 1.0 + 0.0001 = 1.0 in FP16. The weight never changes.

title: FP8 Delayed Scaling Out-of-Bounds in Production
scenario: Your serving infrastructure processes 5,000 QPS using an FP8 E4M3 LLM. To avoid the computational overhead of calculating the absolute maximum of every activation tensor dynamically, you implement 'delayed scaling', using the max scale from the previous N batches. During a sudden shift in user prompts (e.g., switching from English to code generation), the model outputs NaNs.
common_mistake: Assuming the new prompt distribution has longer sequence lengths that are causing out-of-memory errors in the KV cache.
realistic_solution: Delayed scaling relies on the assumption that activation distributions change smoothly between consecutive batches. When the prompt distribution shifts abruptly, the actual maximum activation value in the new batch can be significantly larger than the historical maximum used to calculate the scaling factor. Because E4M3 has a very low maximum representable value, applying a scaling factor optimized for smaller historical activations to the new, larger activations pushes them past the format's limit, causing an overflow to NaN. The architectural fix is to either use a safe margin in the delayed
napkin_math: History max = 10.0. E4M3 max = 448. Scale S = 448 / 10 = 44.8. New batch has an activation outlier of 15.0. Quantized value = 15.0 * 44.8 = 672. Since 672 > 448, it overflows the E4M3 range and becomes NaN.

title: RoPE Embedding Degradation in BF16
scenario: You are extending a 13B model's context window from 8k to 100k tokens. You deploy the model purely in BF16 to save memory. While short-context tasks work perfectly, retrieval tasks at position > 60,000 fail entirely. Profiling shows the attention maps become smeared and indistinct at high positions.

realistic_solution: The issue is a direct result of BF16's severe lack of mantissa precision (only 7 bits). Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) relies on calculating cos(m * theta) and sin(m * theta) where m is the token position. At large positions (e.g., m = 60,000), representing 'm' in BF16 causes severe truncation. The step size between representable numbers in BF16 at this magnitude is large enough that consecutive token positions map to the exact same BF16 value. Consequently, the model cannot distinguish between adjacent tokens at long contexts. The architecture must compute and apply RoPE in FP32, casting to

title: Mitigating W8A8 Activation Outliers via Mathematical Migration

Because the linear operation Y = X * W is equivalent to Y = (X / S) * (W * S) for any diagonal matrix S, you can migrate the quantization difficulty from the activations to the weights. By calculating a per-channel scale factor S based on the ratio of activation maximums to weight maximums (typically S_i = max(|X_i|)^0.5 / max(|W_i|)^0.5), you divide the activation outliers by S and multiply the corresponding weights by S. title: MoE Router Logit Overflow in FP16
scenario: You are training an 8x22B Mixture of Experts (MoE) model using FP16. When a logit exceeds FP16's maximum representable value (65504), it overflows to Infinity. If multiple experts get an Infinity logit (or if the implementation subtracts the max, resulting in Inf - Inf = NaN), the resulting softmax outputs NaNs or uniform probabilities (if NaNs are masked to 1/N). title: BF16 Accumulation Precision Loss in Massive GEMMs

In a 16384-dimension dot product, you are accumulating 16,384 products into a single sum. As the running sum grows larger, the precision gap (machine epsilon) scales up. Eventually, the sum becomes so large that adding the next small product falls entirely below the precision threshold, effectively adding zero (swamping). title: INT4 KV Cache Group Size Architecture
scenario: To serve a 70B model with a massive batch size, you implement INT4 KV cache, shrinking the cache from 80GB to 20GB. Outliers do not just occur in specific feature channels; they also occur at specific token positions (e.g., punctuation or start-of-sequence tokens). If you quantize an entire channel across a long sequence length (e.g., 4096 tokens) using a single scale factor, one temporal outlier in that channel will force a massive scale factor, crushing the precision of the other 4095 tokens in that channel into the tiny 16-value INT4 space. title: Dynamic Loss Scaling in Mixed Precision

If an overflow occurs, the optimizer skips the update, reduces the scale factor (usually by dividing by 2), and continues. This dynamically finds the optimal window between underflow and over

title: Large Vocabulary Cross-Entropy Overflow
scenario: You are pre-training a multilingual LLM with a massive vocabulary size of 256,000 tokens and a global batch size of 4096. To optimize memory, you configure the final language modeling head and the cross-entropy loss calculation to run entirely in FP16. Training collapses to NaN loss within the first 10 steps.

realistic_solution: The collapse is due to numeric overflow in the denominator of the Softmax function within the Cross-Entropy loss. With a vocabulary of 256,000, you are summing 256,000 exponential terms. Even if the pre-softmax logits are perfectly centered and average to 0 (where exp(0) = 1), the sum of 256,000 ones is 256,000. This massively exceeds the FP16 maximum of 65504, causing the denominator to evaluate to Infinity, and the loss to evaluate to NaN. The architecture must enforce that the final logits are upcast to FP32, and the cross-entropy loss/softmax is computed purely in FP32.
napkin_math: Sum(exp(logits)) for 256k tokens. If average exp(logit) = 0.5, Sum = 128,000. 128,000 > 65504 -> Inf in FP16. Log(Softmax) becomes Log(exp(x) / Inf) = Log(0) = -Inf. Loss = NaN.

title: Activation-Aware Weight Quantization (AWQ)
scenario: Deploying a 70B model on 2x 24GB GPUs requires W4A16 (INT4 weight, FP16 activation) quantization. Standard Round-to-Nearest (RTN) INT4 degrades zero-shot accuracy by 15%. However, applying AWQ restores accuracy to within 1% of the FP16 baseline without changing the inference kernel or the 4-bit data type.

Quantization errors on these specific weights are amplified by the massive activations, destroying the output. AWQ identifies these salient weight channels by profiling the activations. Quantized = round(0.08/0.05)*0.05 = 0.1. Effective w after descaling = 0.1 / 4 = 0.025. Error reduced from 100% to 25%.

title: FP8 KV Cache Asymmetry: K vs V Degradation
scenario: You optimize a 100B model by storing the KV cache in FP8 E4M3. Throughput increases dramatically. However, you notice that while storing the Value (V) cache in E4M3 causes no measurable degradation, storing the Key (K) cache in E4M3 causes a 5% drop in MMLU accuracy.

The K cache is used in a dot product with the Query (Q * K^T), and the result is immediately passed through a Softmax function. Softmax exponentiates the values, meaning even tiny quantization errors in the K cache are exponentially amplified, severely disrupting the attention probability distribution. Conversely, the V cache is merely linearly combined using those attention probabilities (Prob * V). title: MoE Router Overhead in FP8 Inference
scenario: You are serving a 140B MoE model. The entire model (weights and activations) is quantized to FP8 E4M3. However, because MoE router logits overflow the E4M3 range, the architecture requires casting activations to E5M2 just for the router, then back to E4M3. This casting introduces a 15ms latency penalty per generated token.

The memory bandwidth saved by keeping the router inputs in FP8 is negligible, but the latency cost of dispatching casting kernels is high. The correct architecture is to keep the router inputs, weights, and computation entirely in higher precision (BF16 or FP16). The memory bandwidth difference is imperceptible, but launching 2 casting kernels (upcast, downcast) across 32 layers takes 32 * 2 * 0.2ms = 12.8ms of pure kernel launch overhead.

title: LLM Serving Arithmetic Intensity
scenario: You are serving a 30B parameter LLM (FP16) on a single 80GB A100 GPU (2 TB/s HBM bandwidth). At a batch size of 1, token generation latency is 30ms, yielding ~33 QPS. The 30B FP16 weights consume 60GB of HBM, leaving 20GB. At 2048 tokens, the KV cache per request is ~5GB, limiting the maximum batch size to 3. To hit 500 QPS, you must optimize the memory footprint. By applying INT4 Weight-Only Quantization (W4A16), the weight footprint drops from 60GB to 15GB. This frees up 45GB of VRAM, allowing a batch size of up to 12. Furthermore, reading 15GB of weights at 2 TB/s takes only 7.5ms per token. At a batch size of 10, the theoretical
napkin_math: KV cache per token (assuming 64 layers, 64 heads, 128 dim): 2 * 64 * 64 * 128 * 2 bytes = 2.1 MB. At 2048 tokens: 2.1 MB * 2048 = 4.3 GB per request. FP16 Weights: 30B * 2B = 60GB. Max BS = (80GB - 60GB) / 4.3GB = 4 (OOMs at 4 due to context/activations overhead). Max safe BS = 3. Throughput = 3 / (60GB / 2000GB/s) = 3 / 0.03s = 100 QPS. INT4 Weights: 30B * 0.5B = 15GB. Remaining VRAM = 65GB. Max BS = 65 / 4.3 = 15. Read latency = 15GB / 2000GB/s = 7.5ms. Throughput at BS 10 = 10 / 0.0075s = 1333 QPS.

title: Mitigating KV Cache Fragmentation
scenario: You are deploying a 13B parameter LLM for a multi-tenant chat application on a 40GB A100. The FP16 weights consume 26GB. You pre-allocate a static, contiguous KV cache buffer for 8 concurrent requests, assuming a maximum sequence length of 2048 tokens. Under production load, average sequence lengths are only 256 tokens, meaning actual memory utilized is low, but the system cannot accept more than 8 concurrent requests without OOMing, resulting in terrible serving infrastructure cost efficiency.

To optimize memory capacity and increase batch size, implement PagedAttention. This technique breaks the KV cache into fixed-size non-contiguous physical blocks (e.g., 16 tokens per block) managed by a block table, decoupling logical sequence length from physical memory allocation. This completely eliminates external fragmentation and bounds internal fragmentation to at most one block per sequence. By allocating physical memory dynamically o
napkin_math: KV cache size per token: 2 (K,V) * 40 layers * 40 heads * 128 dim * 2 bytes = 819 KB. Static allocation for 2048 tokens: 2048 * 819 KB = 1.67 GB per request. Max requests in 14GB VRAM = 14 GB / 1.67 GB = 8 requests. Wasted memory per average request (256 tokens): (2048 - 256) * 819 KB = 1.46 GB wasted (87% waste). With PagedAttention, memory per average request: 256 * 819 KB = ~210 MB. Max concurrent requests supported = 14 GB / 210 MB = ~66 requests. Throughput scales by over 8x.

title: Optimizer State NVMe Offloading
scenario: You are fine-tuning a 70B parameter model on an 8x 80GB A100 node (640GB total HBM) using DeepSpeed ZeRO-Infinity. The node has 4x PCIe Gen4 NVMe drives providing an aggregate read/write bandwidth of 28 GB/s. Profiling shows that the forward/backward passes take 5 seconds, but the optimizer step takes an agonizing 60 seconds, causing GPUs to sit idle 92% of the time. You must optimize the training loop to reduce this bottleneck.
common_mistake: Increasing the micro-batch size to improve GPU utilization, which increases activation memory and forces even more data to be spilled to NVMe, worsening the I/O bottleneck.
realistic_solution: The 60-second delay is caused by the memory bandwidth bottleneck between the NVMe drives and GPU HBM. A 70B model requires 840GB of memory just for standard Adam optimizer states (FP32 momentum, FP32 variance, FP32 master weights), which exceeds the 640GB HBM and forces NVMe offloading. During the optimizer step, these 840GB must be read from and written back to NVMe. To optimize this, you should switch to an 8-bit optimizer (e.g., 8-bit Adam) to quantize the momentum and variance states, and evaluate offloading to CPU RAM instead of NVMe if available. CPU RAM typically offers much higher band

At 28 GB/s aggregate NVMe bandwidth: 1680 GB / 28 GB/s = 60 seconds (matching the profile). Optimization 1 (8-bit Adam): 2 bytes (states) + 4 bytes (weights) = 6 bytes * 70B = 420 GB. Traffic = 840 GB. Time = 30 seconds (2x speedup). Optimization 2 (CPU RAM Offload): At 200 GB/s CPU memory bandwidth, reading and writing 840 GB takes 1680 GB / 200 GB/s = 8.4 seconds (7x speedup).

title: SRAM Layer Fusion for Edge CNNs
scenario: You are deploying an INT8 vision model on a custom edge accelerator with 4MB of on-chip SRAM (500 GB/s bandwidth) and external LPDDR4 DRAM (10 GB/s bandwidth). Processing a high-resolution block (Conv 3x3 -> ReLU -> Conv 1x1) takes ~26ms per frame. The MAC units can theoretically execute the operations in 2ms. Profiling indicates the chip is stalled waiting on DRAM memory transactions for the intermediate activations between the two convolutions.
common_mistake: Applying further weight quantization (e.g., to INT4), which degrades model accuracy without addressing the actual bottleneck: the massive intermediate activation memory footprint.
realistic_solution: The performance is bottlenecked by the external DRAM bandwidth. The intermediate activation tensor is too large to fit in the 4MB SRAM, forcing the accelerator to write the output of the 3x3 Conv to external DRAM and immediately read it back for the 1x1 Conv. To optimize this, implement Layer Fusion (Depth-First Tiling). By computing a small spatial tile (e.g., 128x128) of the 3x3 Conv, the intermediate activations remain small enough to stay entirely within the 4MB SRAM. The ReLU and 1x1 Conv are applied immediately to this tile in SRAM, and only the final output is written to DRAM. This comp

Total DRAM traffic = 256 MB. Time spent on DRAM I/O = 256 MB / 10 GB/s = 25.6 ms (matching the 26ms total time). With Layer Fusion: Tile size 128x128 * 64 channels = 1 MB. This easily fits in the 4MB SRAM. The intermediate 128 MB is never written to or read from DRAM. DRAM traffic for intermediate activations is reduced to 0 bytes, saving ~25.6 ms and dropping the block latency closer to the compute-bound 2ms limit

title: Diagnosing Flat Ring AllReduce Latency
scenario: You are debugging a distributed training job across 1024 GPUs (128 nodes, 8 GPUs/node). The network provides 400 Gbps (50 GB/s) per node via RoCEv2. Profiling reveals that synchronizing a 100 MB gradient bucket takes ~106 ms. A junior engineer notes that the network bandwidth utilization is barely 2% (~1 GB/s) and suspects faulty transceiver cables or switch throttling. You need to identify the true root cause using latency decomposition.
common_mistake: Assuming hardware throttling or network congestion and trying to tune TCP window sizes or replace cables.
realistic_solution: The symptom of extremely low bandwidth utilization coupled with high latency in large clusters points to the latency term dominating the communication time due to a sub-optimal topology (Flat Ring). By decomposing the Ring AllReduce time into its hop-latency and bandwidth-transfer components, we see that transferring small chunks over many hops causes the network to be entirely latency-bound. In a Flat Ring of N=1024, the algorithm requires 2(N-1) hops. For a 100MB message, the chunk size per hop is tiny (~100 KB). The time spent serializing data is negligible compared to the sheer number of n

Total Time T = 2(N-1) * alpha + 2((N-1)/N) * (M/B). 
For N = 1024, M = 100 MB, B = 50 GB/s (400 Gbps), alpha (hop latency) = 50 us.
Latency term = 2 * 1023 * 50 us = 102.3 ms.
Bandwidth term = 2 * (1023/1024) * 100 MB / 50 GB/s = 3.99 ms.
Total Time = 102.3 ms + 3.99 ms = 106.3 ms.
Bandwidth Utilization = 100 MB / 106.3 ms = ~0.94 GB/s (less than 2% of 50 GB/s).

title: Root-Causing AllToAll Tail Latency
scenario: You are serving a Mixture of Experts (MoE) model on 64 GPUs across 8 nodes connected via a 400 Gbps (50 GB/s) non-blocking RoCEv2 switch. During the expert routing phase, the AllToAll collective (32 MB total payload per GPU) is expected to take ~0.6-1.0 ms. Instead, profiling shows erratic tail latencies of 15-20 ms. Switch telemetry indicates massive spikes in Priority Flow Control (PFC) pause frames and buffer drops.

realistic_solution: The symptoms (high tail latency, PFC pause storms, buffer drops) point directly to a network incast problem. Decomposing the AllToAll latency reveals that while propagation and nominal serialization delays are sub-millisecond, the queueing delay and timeout/retransmission penalties completely dominate. In a flat AllToAll, N-1 GPUs simultaneously send data to 1 target GPU. For 64 GPUs and a 32MB payload, each GPU sends 0.5MB to every other GPU. At the switch, 63 flows of 0.5MB arrive simultaneously for each destination port, creating a massive microburst. This instantly overwhelms the port's bu
napkin_math:
Payload M = 32 MB per GPU, N = 64 GPUs. Chunk per destination = 32 MB / 64 = 0.5 MB.
Microburst at single switch destination port = 63 senders * 0.5 MB = 31.5 MB arriving almost instantly.
Typical switch buffer per port = ~16 MB.
Overflow = 31.5 MB - 16 MB = 15.5 MB of data that cannot be buffered.
Nominal serialization time = 31.5 MB / 50 GB/s = 0.63 ms.
Queueing + PFC pause / Retransmission penalty = ~15 ms (dominating the 0.63 ms serialization time).

title: MoE Interconnect Bottleneck on TPU Pods
scenario: You are training a 1.2T parameter Mixture of Experts (MoE) model (64 experts) on a TPU v5e Pod (256 chips). Symptom: Model Flop Utilization (MFU) is stuck at 15%. Profiling shows 65% of step time is spent in `all-to-all` communication. The batch size per chip is 16, sequence length is 2048, and hidden dimension is 8192.

With 64 experts and a global batch size of 4096 (256*16), each expert only receives ~64 sequences per step. The small payload size fails to saturate the ICI bandwidth, dominated instead by latency and packet headers. napkin_math: Tokens per chip = 16 * 2048 = 32,768. Total tokens = 256 * 32,768 = 8,388,608. Tokens per expert (assuming uniform routing) = 8,388,608 / 64 = 131,072. Payload per expert = 131,072 * 8192 (dim) * 2 bytes (BF16) = 2.14 GB. Over 256 chips, each chip sends ~8.3 MB to every other chip. TPU v5e ICI bandwidth is ~400 GB/s. Theoretical transfer time = 8.3 MB / 400 GB/s = 20 microseconds. However, 256 peer-to-peer connections of 8.3 MB incur massive TCP/routing overhead on the torus network, achieving <10% of peak bandwidth.
