We apply a deterministic greedy algorithm or a lightweight linear program to select the final 10 items such that creator demographic constraints are met while maximizing the
napkin_math:
SLA: 60ms. Ranker takes 40ms. Budget remaining: 20ms (including network/serialization, leaving ~10ms for compute).
Re-ranking 1000 items with O(N log N) sorting: 1000 * 10 ≈ 10,000 operations. With complex fairness constraints, an LP solver on 1000 items takes ~25-40ms (violates SLA).
Truncating to top 100 items: LP solver on 100 items takes ~1-2ms. 40ms (rank) + 2ms (re-rank) = 42ms. Leaves 18ms for overhead, safely meeting the 60ms SLA.

title: Pipelined Guardrails in Compound Systems
scenario: You are deploying a financial compliance RAG API that must return a complete JSON payload. The SLA for P90 latency is 1000ms. Current sequential pipeline: Embedding & Vector Retrieval (250ms) -> Main LLM Generation for avg 150 tokens (600ms) -> Output Compliance Guardrail evaluating the full text (300ms). Total P90 is 1150ms. You cannot change the models, replace the guardrail, or return partial/streamed responses to the client. The guardrail latency scales linearly with token count (i.e., 50 tokens = 100ms). Design an orchestration architecture to meet the 1000ms SLA.
common_mistake: Suggesting to stream the response directly to the client to reduce perceived latency, which violates the strict requirement to return a complete JSON payload.
realistic_solution:
Implement a Pipelined/Chunked Guardrail architecture (a core Compound System Pattern). Instead of waiting for the main LLM to emit all 150 tokens before starting the 300ms guardrail check, orchestrate the system to evaluate chunks asynchronously via an internal stream.

1. Decomposition: The LLM generates 150 tokens in 600ms (4ms/token). Assuming chunks of 50 tokens (approx. one sentence), a chunk is completed every 200ms.
2. Pipelining: Stream the LLM output to an internal orchestrator. Once Chunk 1 (50 tokens) is generated at T=450ms (250ms retrieval + 200ms generation), dispatch it to a Gua
napkin_math:
Sequential Critical Path: 250ms (DB) + 600ms (LLM) + 300ms (Guardrail) = 1150ms.

Pipelined Critical Path:
- T=0: Start Retrieval
- T=250: Retrieval done, Start LLM Gen
- T=450: LLM yields Chunk 1. Start Guardrail(C1).
- T=550: Guardrail(C1) finishes.
- T=650: LLM yields Chunk 2. Start Guardrail(C2).
- T=750: Guardrail(C2) finishes.
- T=850: LLM yields Chunk 3 (Final). Start Guardrail(C3).
- T=950: Guardrail(C3) finishes. All checks passed.

Total latency = 950ms. This yields a 200ms reduction, safely fitting within the 1000ms SLA.

title: RoCEv2 Deadlock in Drone Swarm
scenario: A localized edge cluster of 16 autonomous drone base stations relies on RoCEv2 over a 25GbE switch to perform real-time distributed SLAM. During heavy map synchronization (bursts of 50MB per node), the entire cluster's network throughput suddenly drops to 0 Mbps and requires a hard switch reset to recover.
common_mistake: Assuming switch buffer overflow caused TCP-like backoff or packet loss.
realistic_solution: Diagnose Priority Flow Control (PFC) deadlock. RoCEv2 relies on a lossless network achieved via PFC. When nodes send bursty all-to-all traffic (like map sync), circular buffer dependencies form across switch ports. A pause frame from node A pauses the switch port, which pauses node B, which is pausing the switch, creating a cyclic dependency. The solution is to configure PFC watchdog timers to drop packets breaking the deadlock, or move to a lossy RDMA protocol (like relying on ECN/DCQCN alone) since edge networks struggle with strict lossless guarantees.
napkin_math: 16 nodes * 50MB burst = 800MB total burst. 25GbE = ~3.125GB/s. Typical edge switch buffer is 2-4MB. 800MB >> 4MB, meaning PFC pause frames are guaranteed to trigger. Without deadlock prevention, the probability of a circular dependency approaches 100% during simultaneous all-to-all bursts.

title: Camera Incast on Factory Floor
scenario: A factory floor edge deployment has 100 high-resolution QA cameras connected via 1GbE to a single 10GbE edge server. Every 100ms, all cameras simultaneously send a 200KB compressed image tensor for central inference. The average latency is expected to be <5ms, but P99 latency frequently spikes >200ms, causing pipeline stalls.
common_mistake: Blaming the edge server's CPU or PCIe bus for not ingesting data fast enough.
realistic_solution: Identify TCP Incast. 100 cameras sending 200KB simultaneously creates a synchronized micro-burst. A standard edge switch has shared packet buffers (often 2MB-4MB). The synchronized burst overwhelms the switch egress buffer toward the 10GbE server port. The switch drops packets, forcing TCP retransmission timeouts (RTO), which default to 200ms in many Linux distributions. The fix is to introduce jitter (randomized offsets) to camera transmissions, increase switch buffers, or tune the TCP RTO minimum down to 1-5ms.
napkin_math: Burst size = 100 * 200KB = 20,000KB = 20MB. Typical edge switch buffer = 4MB. 20MB > 4MB. Egress port drops ~16MB of packets. Time to transmit 20MB at 10Gbps (~1.25GB/s) should be 20MB / 1.25GB/s = 16ms. Latency jumps from 16ms to 200ms+ due to TCP RTO.

title: Harsh Environment Signal Degradation
scenario: An onboard ML system in an electric mining haul truck uses 100GbE PAM4 copper DAC cables to connect a sensor-fusion node to an inference accelerator. Throughput remains at 80Gbps, but interconnect latency randomly spikes from 500ns to 50us whenever the truck's main electric drive motors accelerate. OS reports 0 dropped packets.
common_mistake: Investigating OS thread scheduling, NUMA effects, or thermal throttling during acceleration.
realistic_solution: Diagnose PHY-level Forward Error Correction (FEC) intervention due to Electromagnetic Interference (EMI). 100GbE over PAM4 requires FEC (like RS-FEC) to maintain link integrity. When the electric motors draw massive current, they generate high EMI, degrading signal integrity on the copper DACs and causing a high Bit Error Rate (BER). The MAC layer recovers the data using FEC, which prevents OS-visible packet loss but adds significant processing latency (correcting symbols takes time). The solution is to replace copper DACs with Active Optical Cables (AOCs) which are immune to EMI.
napkin_math: Normal 100GbE PHY latency ~ 100-200ns. RS-FEC adds ~250ns base latency, but heavy symbol correction introduces pipeline stalls at the hardware level, pushing latency to 50us. At 100Gbps (12.5GB/s), a 50us stall delays 625KB of buffered data per spike.

title: MEC Rack Bisection Bandwidth Collapse
scenario: A 5G Multi-access Edge Computing (MEC) rack uses a 2-tier Fat-Tree topology. 16 Leaf switches connect to 4 Spine switches. Each leaf has 16x 100GbE downlinks to edge servers. Each leaf only has 4x 100GbE uplinks to the spines. During a federated learning job across 64 nodes (spread evenly across 4 leaves), all-reduce bandwidth drops to exactly 25% of expected.
common_mistake: Assuming the Ring All-Reduce algorithm is implemented incorrectly or CPUs are bottlenecking.
realistic_solution: Root cause is topological oversubscription. The leaf switches have 16 downlinks (1.6 Tbps) but only 4 uplinks (400 Gbps), creating a 4:1 oversubscription ratio. When nodes on different leaves communicate during the all-reduce phase, traffic must traverse the spine. Since all 64 nodes participate simultaneously, the uplinks are perfectly saturated at a 4:1 ratio. The solution is to map the distributed training job to nodes within the same leaf switch (localizing traffic) or upgrade the uplinks to achieve a 1:1 non-blocking bisection bandwidth.
napkin_math: Leaf downlinks = 16 * 100Gbps = 1600 Gbps. Leaf uplinks = 4 * 100Gbps = 400 Gbps. Oversubscription ratio = 1600 / 400 = 4:1. Maximum inter-leaf bandwidth per node = 100Gbps / 4 = 25Gbps. This perfectly explains the exact 25% performance degradation.

title: Adversarial Traffic on Edge Dragonfly
scenario: A specialized edge cluster for autonomous vehicle fleet management uses a miniature Dragonfly topology connecting 72 nodes across 9 chassis (8 nodes per chassis). Intra-chassis links are 200Gbps; inter-chassis optical links are 100Gbps. A distributed graph neural network (GNN) inference task causes massive congestion on a single inter-chassis link, while other optical links sit at 0% utilization.
common_mistake: Blaming the GNN's graph partitioning and attempting to manually re-partition the graph.
realistic_solution: Identify the failure of static minimal routing in a Dragonfly topology. Dragonfly relies on high-radix routers and sparse global links. If static minimal (shortest-path) routing is used, an adversarial traffic pattern (like a GNN where one chassis communicates heavily with exactly one other chassis) will bottleneck on the single dedicated global link between those two chassis. The solution is to enable Adaptive Routing (AR) or Valiant Load Balancing (VLB), which routes traffic non-minimally through intermediate chassis to utilize idle global links and increase effective bisection bandwidth.
napkin_math: In a 9-group Dragonfly, each group has 1 direct link to every other group. Traffic from Group A to Group B uses 1 link (100Gbps). With VLB, Group A can route to Group B via the 7 other groups. Effective bandwidth = 1 direct link + 7 indirect paths = 8 * 100Gbps = 800Gbps. Static routing leaves 700Gbps of potential bandwidth unused.

title: ECMP Hash Polarization in Edge Storage
scenario: An edge storage rack uses 4 parallel 100GbE links between a Top-of-Rack (ToR) switch and a dense NVMe-oF storage array to provide 400Gbps aggregate bandwidth. However, when 4 edge inference servers pull 100Gbps video streams simultaneously, total throughput caps at 100Gbps. One specific 100GbE link shows 100% utilization while the other 3 show near 0%.
common_mistake: Assuming the storage array's NVMe controllers are internally bottlenecked at 100Gbps.
realistic_solution: Diagnose Equal-Cost Multi-Path (ECMP) hash polarization/collision. ECMP distributes traffic across parallel links by hashing packet header fields (typically 5-tuple: Src IP, Dst IP, Protocol, Src Port, Dst Port). If the 4 inference servers connect to a single IP/Port on the storage array and use fixed source ports, or if the switch's hash algorithm only checks IP addresses, the hashes for all 4 flows compute to the same output port modulo 4. The solution is to vary the source ports (multiplexing flows), use a deeper hashing algorithm, or implement flowlet switching.
napkin_math: 4 flows mapping across 4 links. Output port = hash(flow_i) % 4. If all hashes result in the same modulo (e.g., 0), all traffic goes to Link 0. Maximum throughput = 1 link * 100Gbps = 100Gbps. Wasted bandwidth = 3 links * 100Gbps = 300Gbps. System operates at 25% efficiency.

title: FP8 Gradient Casting Overflow Crisis
scenario: You are migrating a 70B parameter LLM fine-tuning pipeline from FP16 to FP8 on H100 GPUs to double throughput to 4,000 tokens/sec/GPU. The forward pass works perfectly, but during the backward pass in QAT, the loss immediately spikes to NaN after 15 steps. The gradient norms are roughly ~800.0 before the crash. What caused this catastrophic failure?
common_mistake: Assuming FP8 has uniform dynamic range and attributing the NaN to a learning rate that is too high for quantized training.
realistic_solution: The root cause is a mismatch in FP8 encoding formats. FP8 has two standard formats: E4M3 (4 exponent bits, 3 mantissa bits) and E5M2 (5 exponent bits, 2 mantissa bits). You used E4M3 for both the forward and backward passes. E4M3 provides higher precision (needed for activations/weights) but has a maximum representable value of 448. When gradients hit ~800.0, E4M3 overflows, producing NaNs. The fix is to use E4M3 for the forward pass (activations and weights) and E5M2 for the backward pass (gradients), as E5M2 sacrifices precision for a much larger dynamic range (max value 57,344), comfortably

title: Cross-Lingual PTQ Activation Outliers
scenario: A globally deployed 30B LLM is quantized using INT8 W8A8 PTQ. English generation latency drops from 45ms/token to 22ms/token with <0.5% perplexity degradation. However, telemetry shows that German and Code generation outputs are producing gibberish, with perplexity spiking by 400%. Your calibration dataset consisted of 5,000 Wikipedia articles. Why did quantization break specific languages?
common_mistake: Assuming the tokenizer vocabulary distribution caused the issue or that INT8 inherently lacks the precision for complex syntax.
realistic_solution: The root cause is uncalibrated activation outliers. Large language models exhibit massive activation outliers in specific feed-forward channels. Your PTQ calibration dataset used 5,000 English Wikipedia articles, so the static quantization scales for activations were set based on English outlier magnitudes. German and Code inputs route through different network pathways, producing activation outliers up to 10x larger than English ones in specific channels. Because the PTQ ranges were statically fixed to the English maximums, these cross-lingual outliers were severely clipped, destroying the si
napkin_math: English Max Act = 15.0. INT8 Scale = 15.0 / 127 = 0.118 per bin. German Max Act = 120.0. With static scale, 120.0 is clipped to 15.0 (losing 87.5% of the signal magnitude). If calibrated on German: Scale = 120.0 / 127 = 0.94 per bin, meaning English activations (max 15.0) only use 15.0 / 0.94 = 16 bins (3-4 bits of precision), degrading English.

title: Asymmetric Quantization Throughput Collapse
scenario: You quantize a recommendation model's MLP layers to INT8 to deploy on Intel Xeon CPUs. Model size drops from 4GB to 1GB. However, CPU inference throughput drops from 12,000 QPS (FP32) to 5,500 QPS (INT8). Profiling shows the CPU is 100% utilized and not memory-bound. You used asymmetric per-tensor quantization (min/max). Why is the smaller INT8 model slower than FP32?
common_mistake: Assuming the CPU lacks INT8 vector instructions (VNNI) or that memory bandwidth is the bottleneck due to cache thrashing.
realistic_solution: The root cause is the computational overhead of asymmetric quantization zero-points. CPU SIMD instructions (like AVX-512 VNNI) are optimized for INT8 dot products. However, with asymmetric quantization, the matrix multiplication expands: (W - Zw) * (X - Zx) = W*X - W*Zx - X*Zw + Zw*Zx. While Zw*Zx can be precomputed, the cross terms (W*Zx and X*Zw) require a reduction across the rows/columns at runtime. This forces the CPU to execute multiple additional instructions (accumulations and broadcasts) per MAC, destroying the throughput. Switching to symmetric quantization (zero-point = 0) eliminate
napkin_math: Symmetric MAC: 1 instruction (VPMADDUBSW). Asymmetric MAC: W*X (1 inst) + row_sum(W)*Zx (1 inst) + col_sum(X)*Zw (1 inst) + precomputed_term. Execution time per layer increases by roughly ~3x in instruction count, turning a 1ms FP32 layer into a 0.33ms (INT8 sym) or a 1.2ms (INT8 asym) layer.

title: The QAT Shadow Weight OOM
scenario: You initiate Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) for a 15B parameter vision-language model on an 8x80GB A100 node. Standard FP16 fine-tuning takes 45GB VRAM per GPU. When you enable INT8 QAT, you expect memory to drop or stay constant, but it spikes to 82GB per GPU, causing an Out-Of-Memory (OOM) error. What is causing this massive memory inflation?
common_mistake: Believing that QAT actually stores the model in INT8 during training, and attributing the OOM to a memory leak in the data loader.
realistic_solution: The root cause is the maintenance of FP32 'shadow weights' alongside the fake-quantization graph. In QAT, gradients cannot flow through discrete INT8 steps (due to zero derivatives), so the Straight-Through Estimator (STE) is used. This requires keeping the continuous FP32 weights to accumulate small gradient updates. During the forward pass, the framework must store both the FP32 weights AND the simulated INT8 weights (FakeQuant), plus the FP32 optimizer states (Adam requires momentums in FP32). Furthermore, FakeQuant nodes inject additional activation tensors into the computation graph that
napkin_math: 15B params. Standard FP16 FT: Weights 30GB + Adam (FP32) 60GB = 90GB (usually sharded via FSDP to ~15GB/GPU) + 30GB activations = 45GB. QAT adds: FP32 shadow weights (60GB total, ~10GB/GPU sharded) + INT8 fake weights (15GB total, 2GB/GPU) + FakeQuant activation copies (~25GB/GPU overhead). Total per GPU: 45GB + 10GB + 2GB + 25GB = 82GB -> OOM.

title: Depthwise Conv Per-Tensor Collapse
scenario: You deploy an INT8 quantized Conformer acoustic model for global speech recognition. The pipeline processes 5,000 hours of audio daily. After switching from FP32 to INT8 using per-tensor PTQ, the Word Error Rate (WER) jumps from 4.2% to 28.5%. Standard Convs show no degradation, but the Depthwise Convolution layers exhibit massive activation errors. Why?
common_mistake: Assuming the calibration dataset lacked acoustic diversity, leading to clipping of speech outliers.
realistic_solution: The root cause is applying per-tensor quantization to Depthwise Convolutions. In standard convolutions, weights are mixed across input channels, keeping dynamic ranges relatively uniform. In Depthwise Convolutions, each channel is convolved independently. The network learns wildly different weight magnitudes for different channels (e.g., channel 0 might have a range of [-0.1, 0.1] while channel 120 has [-50.0, 50.0]). With per-tensor quantization, a single scale factor is calculated based on the global maximum (50.0). Small-magnitude channels are entirely squashed to zero because their entire
napkin_math: Global max = 50.0. Per-tensor INT8 scale = 50.0 / 127 = 0.393. Channel 0 range is [-0.1, 0.1]. Number of representable bins for Channel 0 = (0.1 - (-0.1)) / 0.393 = 0.5 bins. All weights in Channel 0 quantize to exactly 0. 100% information loss for that channel.

title: Long-Context KV Cache RoPE Failure
scenario: To increase serving batch size from 32 to 128, you quantize the KV cache of a 7B LLM to INT8, saving 12GB of VRAM. Short-context tasks (1k tokens) maintain 99% accuracy. However, a 16k-token needle-in-a-haystack passkey test completely fails, dropping from 100% to 4% accuracy. What interaction destroyed the long-context retrieval?
common_mistake: Assuming INT8 simply doesn't have enough bits to store 16,000 unique token representations, leading to hash collisions in attention.
realistic_solution: The root cause is the destructive interaction between INT8 quantization and Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) when quantizing *after* rotation. RoPE injects positional information by rotating the Query and Key vectors. For long contexts, high-frequency dimensions encode fine-grained distances and have very small activation amplitudes. Conversely, LLMs have massive magnitude outliers in specific hidden dimensions. If you apply RoPE and then quantize to INT8 per-tensor or per-token, the large outliers force a large quantization step size. The small-amplitude high-frequency RoPE rotations are r
napkin_math: Max token outlier magnitude = 12.0. INT8 Step size = 12.0 / 127 = 0.094. High-frequency RoPE rotation alters values by ~0.02. Since 0.02 < 0.094/2 (the rounding threshold), the rotation is rounded away (quantized delta = 0). Tokens at pos 10,000 and 15,000 become positionally indistinguishable.

title: The Unfolded BatchNorm Latency Trap
scenario: You deploy an INT8 ResNet-152 image embedding model via TensorRT. Despite the INT8 weights bringing the memory down from 230MB to 58MB, the end-to-end latency is 14ms per image—exactly the same as the FP32 model. The hardware has dedicated INT8 matrix multipliers (Tensor Cores) capable of 4x speedups. Why is there zero latency improvement?
common_mistake: Blaming the PCIe bus transfer time for hiding the compute speedup, or assuming the model is completely memory-bandwidth bound.
realistic_solution: The root cause is a failure to fold Batch Normalization (BN) layers into the Convolutional layers before quantization. In FP32, Conv -> BN -> ReLU is executed sequentially. If BN is not mathematically folded into the Conv weights/biases before the INT8 conversion, the graph compiler is forced to execute: INT8 Conv -> Dequantize to FP32 -> FP32 BN -> Quantize to INT8 -> INT8 ReLU. The massive overhead of inserting hundreds of Quantize/Dequantize nodes and executing memory-bound BN operations in FP32 completely erases the compute savings of the INT8 Convolutions. Folding BN updates the Conv weig
napkin_math: ResNet-152 has ~150 Conv+BN blocks. INT8 Conv takes 0.02ms (150 * 0.02 = 3ms). Unfolded overhead: Dequantize (0.01ms) + FP32 BN (0.05ms) + Quantize (0.01ms) = 0.07ms per block. 150 * 0.07ms = 10.5ms overhead. Total INT8 = 13.5ms. FP32 Conv (0.08ms) + FP32 BN (0.01ms folded) = ~13.5ms. The Quant/Dequant overhead equals the FP32 compute time.

title: NPU Underutilization in Depthwise Convolutions
scenario: You are deploying a MobileNetV2-based object detector on a custom edge NPU featuring a 16x16 systolic array (peak 512 MACs/cycle). The NPU has 2MB of SRAM and runs at 500 MHz. Profiling reveals that the standard 1x1 convolutions achieve 85% array utilization, but the 3x3 depthwise convolutions drop to roughly 6% utilization, causing the overall inference latency to miss the 15ms target by taking 42ms.
common_mistake: Assuming the memory bandwidth is the bottleneck and trying to quantize the depthwise weights to INT4.
realistic_solution: The root cause is a fundamental mismatch between the model's arithmetic intensity and the systolic array's spatial architecture. Depthwise convolutions process each input channel independently, meaning there is zero data reuse across channels. A 16x16 systolic array typically maps input channels to one dimension and output channels to the other. For a depthwise convolution, only 1 out of 16 rows/columns is active, wasting the rest of the array. To resolve this, you must co-design the architecture: either modify the model to use grouped convolutions (where group size matches the array dimension
napkin_math: Systolic array capacity: 16x16 = 256 MAC units. Standard 1x1 conv mapping (Input Channels x Output Channels): perfectly fills the 16x16 grid. Depthwise conv mapping: 1 Input Channel -> 1 Output Channel. Active MACs per cycle = 1x16 (if spatial mapped 1D) or just 1x1. If mapped naively on the channel dimension, utilization = 16 / 256 = 6.25%.

title: FPGA DSP Slice Mapping for INT4
scenario: You are designing an FPGA-based accelerator for a 2W smart camera. The system needs to run a 15-million parameter CNN at 30 FPS. To fit the tight 1.5 GB/s LPDDR4 bandwidth budget, you quantize the model weights to INT4. However, after synthesis, the FPGA routing is severely congested, clock frequency drops from 300MHz to 120MHz, and power consumption exceeds 3W, despite using fewer bits.
common_mistake: Adding more pipeline registers to fix the clock frequency, which worsens area and power.
realistic_solution: The routing congestion and frequency drop occur because the FPGA's hardened DSP slices are natively designed for 8x8 or 18x18 bit arithmetic. When you explicitly force INT4 MAC operations, the synthesis tool often bypasses the hardened, power-efficient DSP slices and implements the INT4 multipliers using general-purpose LUTs (Look-Up Tables) and distributed routing. This consumes massive programmable logic area, causing routing congestion, dropping the clock frequency, and spiking dynamic power. The hardware-software co-design solution is to store the weights in DRAM as INT4 (to save the criti
napkin_math: Memory bandwidth needed at INT8: 15M weights * 1 byte * 30 FPS = 450 MB/s per read (often read multiple times if not cached perfectly). INT4 reduces this to 225 MB/s. DSP slice dynamic power is typically 0.1-0.2x the power of an equivalent LUT-based multiplier. By unpacking INT4->INT8 locally, we save ~1W of LUT switching power.

title: Dataflow Bottleneck in High-Res Drone NPU
scenario: You are programming an autonomous drone's custom ASIC to run a 4K (3840x2160) object detection CNN at 60 FPS. The ASIC has 4MB of on-chip SRAM and a peak DRAM bandwidth of 25.6 GB/s. The compiler defaults to a Weight-Stationary (WS) dataflow. In early layers, the ASIC achieves only 12 FPS, and power monitoring shows the DRAM interface drawing maximum power.
common_mistake: Downsampling the input image to 1080p, sacrificing the required detection accuracy for small objects.
realistic_solution: The Weight-Stationary (WS) dataflow keeps weights pinned in the Processing Element (PE) registers while streaming activations from DRAM through the array. For a 4K image, the input activations of the early layers are massive, while the weight matrices (e.g., 3x3x3x32 = 864 bytes) are tiny. The 4MB SRAM cannot hold the entire 4K feature map, forcing the ASIC to constantly spill and reload activations from DRAM for every output channel computation. The solution is to reconfigure the compiler to use an Output-Stationary (OS) or Row-Stationary (RS) dataflow for the early layers. By tiling the mass
napkin_math: Early layer input size: 3840 x 2160 x 3 channels * 1 byte (INT8) = 24.8 MB. SRAM = 4 MB. In WS dataflow, computing 32 output channels might require streaming the 24.8 MB input map multiple times if it doesn't fit in SRAM. 24.8 MB * 60 FPS = 1.48 GB/s just for one read. If read 10 times due to capacity misses, it's 14.8 GB/s just for layer 1, saturating the 25.6 GB/s bus quickly.

title: MCU SIMD Alignment for Audio CNNs
scenario: You are deploying a keyword spotting CNN on an ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller (features DSP extensions, 32-bit SIMD MAC instructions, 256KB SRAM, 120MHz). The input is a 13-band MFCC spectrogram. Using an INT8 quantized model, inference takes 180ms per window, missing the 50ms real-time deadline. Profiler shows the CMSIS-NN library is executing standard scalar instructions instead of packed SIMD.
common_mistake: Overclocking the MCU or aggressively pruning the model, which hurts accuracy and battery life.
realistic_solution: The Cortex-M4 DSP extensions (like SMLAD) perform dual 16-bit or quad 8-bit MAC operations in a single cycle, but they strictly require data to be 4-byte aligned in memory, and the inner loop dimension (usually channels) must be a multiple of 4. Because the input feature has 13 bands (channels), the CMSIS-NN optimized kernels cannot fully pack the SIMD registers and fall back to the unoptimized scalar C implementation, processing one MAC per cycle instead of four. The co-design solution is to pad the input features from 13 to 16 bands (filling the extra bands with zeros) and ensure all interme
napkin_math: Theoretical compute: 13 channels * scalar MAC = 13 cycles. Padded compute: 16 channels / 4 (SIMD quad MAC) = 4 cycles. Time reduction is 13/4 = 3.25x speedup. Latency drops from 180ms to ~55ms, and with slight loop unrolling, comfortably hits the <50ms target.

title: SmartNIC NPU Arithmetic Intensity Mismatch
scenario: A SmartNIC embedded with a 10 TOPS (INT8) NPU is designed to perform line-rate deep packet inspection at 100 Gbps. The security team deployed an LSTM-based anomaly detection model (batch size 1 per flow). The NPU has 8MB of SRAM with 400 GB/s internal bandwidth. Despite the 10 TOPS rating, the NPU drops 45% of packets, and utilization sits at just 4%.
common_mistake: Trying to increase the NPU clock speed or adding more SRAM capacity to the NPU.
realistic_solution: The LSTM model running at batch size 1 is entirely memory-bandwidth bound due to low arithmetic intensity. An LSTM requires reading the entire weight matrix for every single token (packet payload byte) processed. The arithmetic intensity is approximately 1 MAC (2 operations) per 1 byte of weight read. With an SRAM bandwidth of 400 GB/s, the theoretical maximum throughput is 400 GigaMACs/s (0.8 TOPS), meaning 92% of the 10 TOPS compute array is starved of data. To fix this, you must co-design the algorithm/architecture: replace the LSTM with a 1D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) or a small Tr
napkin_math: NPU Peak Compute: 10 TOPS. SRAM Bandwidth: 400 GB/s. LSTM Arithmetic Intensity: 2 OPs / 1 Byte = 2 OPs/Byte. Max achievable compute = 400 GB/s * 2 OPs/Byte = 800 GOPs = 0.8 TOPS. Utilization = 0.8 / 10 = 8% maximum (often lower due to overhead). To hit 10 TOPS, required intensity = 10,000 GOPs / 400 GB/s = 25 OPs/Byte.

title: Edge TPU Spatial Tiling Overhead
scenario: You are deploying a U-Net based super-resolution model on a Google Edge TPU (8MB SRAM, 4 TOPS, USB 3.0 interface). To fit the 1080p input image, the host CPU spatially tiles the image into 16x16 overlapping patches and sends them to the TPU sequentially. The system targets 30 FPS but only achieves 4 FPS. USB bandwidth monitoring shows 1.8 GB/s of sustained bidirectional transfer.
common_mistake: Compressing the image data over the USB bus using JPEG, which introduces latency and compression artifacts ruining the super-resolution.
realistic_solution: The severe bottleneck is the redundant PCIe/USB host-to-device data transfer caused by naive spatial tiling. Because convolutions have receptive fields, splitting an image into spatial tiles requires overlapping 'halos' to prevent edge artifacts. In a deep U-Net, this halo grows exponentially with depth. If the host tiles the input, it must transmit the massive overlapping regions repeatedly, saturating the USB 3.0 bandwidth. The co-design solution is 'Fused Layer Execution' or 'Depth-First Scheduling' inside the compiler. Instead of host-side tiling, send the entire image (or large chunks) to
napkin_math: 1080p image = 1920x1080x3 = 6.2 MB. USB 3.0 practical bandwidth ~400 MB/s. At 30 FPS, raw input is 186 MB/s. However, with a deep network (e.g., receptive field of 64 pixels), a 128x128 tile needs a 256x256 input patch. Area ratio = 65536 / 16384 = 4x redundancy. 186 MB/s * 4 (input) + output transfers easily exceeds the 400 MB/s limit, dropping framerate to ~4 FPS.

title: Edge GPU LLM KV Cache Thrashing
scenario: You are deploying an 8-billion parameter LLM on a Jetson AGX Orin (64GB unified memory, 204 GB/s bandwidth) for a local coding assistant. Token generation is heavily bandwidth-bound, achieving only 8 tokens/sec. The model weights take 8GB (INT8). The KV cache for a 4096-token context takes about 1GB. The GPU has an 8MB L2 cache. Profiling shows massive L2 cache thrashing during the attention phase of decode.
common_mistake: Quantizing the model weights further to INT4, which won't fix the attention phase bottleneck.
realistic_solution: During auto-regressive decoding, the attention mechanism must read the entire KV cache from DRAM for every single generated token. Even though the 1GB KV cache easily fits in the 64GB unified memory, it is vastly larger than the 8MB L2 cache. This causes a complete L2 cache eviction on every token step, forcing the GPU to pull the 1GB cache across the 204 GB/s memory bus repeatedly. The solution requires algorithmic-hardware co-design: implement Grouped Query Attention (GQA) or Multi-Query Attention (MQA) combined with KV cache quantization (INT8/INT4) or Flash-Decoding. By reducing the size o
napkin_math: Weights: 8GB read per token. KV Cache: 1GB read per token. Total read per token = 9GB. Max theoretical throughput = 204 GB/s / 9 GB/token = 22.6 tokens/sec. Actual is 8 due to overhead. If we use GQA (8x reduction in KV) + INT8 KV (2x reduction), KV cache drops to 1GB / 16 = 62.5 MB. Now memory read per token is ~8.06 GB. Max throughput rises to 204 / 8.06 = 25.3 tokens/sec, and L2 thrashing is mitigated for the KV portion.

title: Activation Outliers in Large Models
scenario: You deploy a 13B parameter LLM for a cloud-based summarization API. To meet the <40ms Time-Between-Tokens (TBT) SLA on L4 GPUs (24GB VRAM, 300 GB/s), you apply INT8 weight and activation quantization (W8A8). However, perplexity degrades severely and the model generates gibberish. Testing shows W8A16 (INT8 weights, FP16 activations) maintains perfect accuracy but misses the latency SLA, clocking in at 55ms TBT. What is the root cause of the W8A8 degradation, and how do you resolve it?
common_mistake: Assuming the weights were quantized incorrectly and switching to QAT, which is expensive and doesn't solve the fundamental activation issue.
realistic_solution: The symptom indicates systematic activation outliers. In LLMs larger than ~6B parameters, massive outliers emerge in specific activation channels (often 100x larger than the median). Standard per-tensor or per-token activation quantization squashes the dynamic range of the non-outlier channels into a single quantization bin, destroying the signal. The solution is to use an algorithm like SmoothQuant, which mathematically migrates the quantization difficulty from the activations to the weights. By scaling down the outlier activation channels and scaling up the corresponding weight channels befo
napkin_math: W8A16 TBT = 55ms. L4 Bandwidth = 300 GB/s. 13B INT8 weights = 13GB. Reading 13GB at 300 GB/s takes ~43ms. The remaining 12ms is compute/overhead. W8A8 uses INT8 Tensor Cores, doubling compute throughput and halving KV cache bandwidth, dropping TBT below the 40ms threshold.

title: FP8 MoE Routing Collapse
scenario: You are pre-training an 8x7B Sparse Mixture of Experts (MoE) model on a cluster of H100s using FP8 mixed precision. At step 15,000, training loss abruptly spikes, and telemetry reveals a routing collapse: 99% of tokens are being sent to Expert 3. You verify the load balancing loss coefficient is correct. The forward pass uses FP8 for all linear layers. What is the cause of this sudden routing degeneration, and how do you fix the precision configuration?
common_mistake: Increasing the capacity factor or load balancing loss weight, ignoring the precision underflow happening in the router.
realistic_solution: The cause is dynamic range mismatch in the FP8 formats. H100 supports two FP8 types: E4M3 (higher precision, lower range, max ~448) and E5M2 (lower precision, higher range, max ~57344). If E4M3 is used for routing logits or gradients, the values can easily underflow to zero or overflow, causing the softmax probabilities in the router to degenerate into one-hot vectors or NaNs, leading to routing collapse. The solution is to cast the MoE router logits and all gradient tensors to E5M2 (or keep them in FP32/BF16, since the router is a tiny fraction of total compute), while keeping the heavy GEMMs
napkin_math: E4M3 max value = 2^(4-1) * (2 - 2^-3) = 8 * 1.875 * 2^4 = 448. Min subnormal = 2^-9. Softmax exponentiation of values outside this narrow band causes immediate underflow/overflow. E5M2 range is up to 57344, preventing this.

title: KV Cache Quantization Sinks
scenario: A 70B parameter LLM deployed for document Q&A uses INT4 weights and an INT8 KV cache to support 32K context windows on an 8xA100 node. For prompts under 1,000 tokens, retrieval accuracy is 95%. However, when prompts exceed 8,000 tokens, the model begins hallucinating and repeating phrases. TBT remains stable at 25ms. Disabling the INT8 KV cache and reverting to FP16 fixes the issue but halves the max concurrency. What causes this long-context degradation?
common_mistake: Assuming RoPE (Rotary Position Embeddings) degrades at long contexts, requiring fine-tuning, when the issue is actually quantization error accumulation.
realistic_solution: The symptom points to accumulated quantization error interacting with 'attention sinks'. In autoregressive LLMs, the first few tokens act as attention sinks, accumulating massive activation magnitudes. Furthermore, RoPE causes phase shifts that vary wildly across context length. Static or per-tensor INT8 quantization of the KV cache forces these massive outlier sink tokens to dictate the quantization scale for the entire sequence. As the context grows to 8K tokens, the subtle relative differences between the thousands of normal tokens are destroyed by the coarse resolution dictated by the sink
napkin_math: 70B model with 64 layers, 8 KV heads, 128 head dim. At 8K tokens: 8000 * 64 * 8 * 128 * 2 = 1.04GB per sequence in INT8. If the sink token has magnitude 50.0 and normal tokens are 0.1, an INT8 step size of 50/127 = 0.39 completely zeros out the normal tokens.

title: QAT Gradient Oscillation
scenario: You are performing Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) to fine-tune an INT4 ResNet-50 variant for a high-throughput edge-cloud hybrid pipeline. You use the Straight-Through Estimator (STE) for the backward pass. Training loss oscillates wildly, and gradient norms spike from ~1.5 to >50 unexpectedly between consecutive batches. The learning rate is set to 1e-3. What is the mechanistic cause of this instability?
common_mistake: Assuming the batch size is too small causing noisy gradients, and increasing it without fixing the underlying STE mismatch.
realistic_solution: The symptom is caused by an impedance mismatch between the Straight-Through Estimator (STE) and the learning rate relative to the quantization step size (delta). In INT4, the weight range is divided into 16 bins. If the weight update (learning rate * gradient) is larger than the distance to the next quantization boundary, the quantized weight jumps to a new bin. Because STE uses coarse approximations, the new bin causes a completely different forward pass, which generates a large opposing gradient in the next step, causing the weight to flip-flop across the boundary. The solution is to drastic
napkin_math: For normalized weights [-1, 1] in INT4 (16 levels), the step size delta = 2 / 15 = 0.133. If LR = 1e-3 and gradient = 150, update = 0.15. Since 0.15 > 0.133, the weight changes bins in a single step, causing massive forward-pass variance.

title: Calibration Overfitting in GPTQ
scenario: A 7B coding assistant is quantized to INT4 using GPTQ to fit on a single 16GB T4 GPU. The resulting model achieves 99% of the FP16 model's accuracy on the Python evaluation suite. However, users report it generates constant syntax errors when writing Java code, despite the FP16 model handling Java perfectly. The GPTQ algorithm used 128 samples from the C4 (Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus) dataset for calibration. Why did Java performance specifically degrade?
common_mistake: Believing INT4 fundamentally lacks the capacity to represent multi-language syntax and switching to a larger bit-width like INT8.
realistic_solution: The cause is calibration data overfitting during the GPTQ process. GPTQ uses second-order information (the inverse Hessian matrix of the activations) to determine which weights are most important to preserve and how to compensate for quantization errors in neighboring weights. Because the calibration dataset (C4) was general English text (and likely heavily skewed toward Python in its code subsets, or simply lacked Java), the Hessian matrix told the algorithm that weights critical for Java syntax were 'unimportant'. Consequently, the quantization error was pushed into those Java-specific weigh
napkin_math: GPTQ updates weights W based on argmin || WX - W_q X ||^2. The Hessian H = X X^T. For a 4096 hidden dim, H is 4096x4096. If Java tokens represent 0% of X, the corresponding dimensions in H are 0, meaning error in those dimensions incurs 0 penalty during the argmin optimization.

title: Unfused Dequantization Bottleneck
scenario: You deploy a 30B parameter LLM on an A100 (80GB, 2 TB/s bandwidth). Using FP16, generation speed is 33 tokens/sec (latency ~30ms/token). You apply W4A16 (INT4 weights, FP16 activations) quantization. You expect a ~4x speedup since generation is memory-bandwidth bound and weights dropped from 60GB to 15GB. Instead, generation speed is 40 tokens/sec (latency ~25ms/token) and GPU compute utilization is 98%. What is the architectural cause of this missing speedup?
common_mistake: Assuming the INT4 decoding math itself (bit-shifting and masking) takes too many ALUs, completely ignoring the memory subsystem traffic.
realistic_solution: The symptom reveals a lack of fused dequantization kernels. In a naive W4A16 implementation, the system launches a standalone kernel that reads the 15GB of INT4 weights from HBM, dequantizes them to FP16, and writes 60GB of FP16 back to HBM. Then, the standard cuBLAS GEMM kernel reads the 60GB of FP16 from HBM to perform the matrix multiplication. This extra memory roundtrip (Read 15GB + Write 60GB + Read 60GB = 135GB) actually moves MORE data than the original FP16 baseline (Read 60GB), completely destroying the bandwidth savings. The high GPU utilization is from memory stall cycles. The solu
napkin_math: FP16 Baseline traffic: 60GB read. Expected INT4 fused traffic: 15GB read (4x less). Actual Unfused traffic: 15G read + 60G write + 60G read = 135GB. Time = 135GB / 2000 GB/s = 67.5ms theoretically, but caching and pipelining masks some of it, resulting in the observed 25ms.

title: Batch Size vs Quantization Pareto Shift
scenario: A cloud inference service migrates from a batch size of 1 to dynamic continuous batching (up to batch size 128) for an 8B model on A100. At batch size 1, the W4A16 quantized model was 3x faster than the FP16 model. However, at batch size 128, the W4A16 model is unexpectedly 20% SLOWER than the FP16 model in total throughput (QPS). What causes this inversion in the performance Pareto curve?
common_mistake: Assuming the KV cache memory size exceeded VRAM capacity at batch size 128, causing spilling to CPU memory.
realistic_solution: The cause is the fundamental shift from a memory-bandwidth bound regime to a compute-bound regime as batch size increases. At batch size 1, the arithmetic intensity is very low; the bottleneck is strictly loading the weights from HBM, so INT4 (which reduces weight bytes by 4x) yields massive speedups. At batch size 128, the arithmetic intensity increases by 128x. The bottleneck is now the math (compute FLOPs). Because A100 Tensor Cores natively support FP16 but do not natively support INT4 * FP16 mixed operations, the W4A16 model must spend ALU cycles dequantizing INT4 to FP16 before feeding i
napkin_math: Arithmetic Intensity (AI) = FLOPs / Bytes. For weights W, Batch B: FLOPs = 2*B*W. Bytes = W. AI = 2*B. At B=1, AI = 2. At B=128, AI = 256. A100 FP16 Compute/Bandwidth ratio = 312 TFLOPs / 2 TB/s = 156 FLOPs/Byte. Since AI (256) > Machine Balance (156), the operation is strictly compute bound. The extra compute required for INT4->FP16 conversion thus degrades performance.

title: TPU Systolic Array Underutilization
scenario: You are migrating a latency-sensitive 7B parameter LLM inference service from A100 GPUs to TPU v4 instances. On A100, at a batch size of 1, the latency is 35ms per token. On TPU v4, the latency spikes to 90ms per token at batch size 1, despite the TPU having higher peak theoretical TFLOPS. Profiling shows TPU Matrix eXecution Unit (MXU) utilization is below 5%.
common_mistake: Assuming the TPU compiler (XLA) failed to fuse operations and manually writing low-level custom kernels.
realistic_solution: The issue is a fundamental mismatch between the algorithm's batch size and the hardware's dataflow architecture. TPUs use a massive 128x128 systolic array (MXU) for matrix multiplications, optimized for high-throughput pipeline execution. At batch size 1, the matrix-vector multiplication cannot physically fill the spatial dimensions of the systolic array, leading to massive underutilization. The tradeoff here is latency versus throughput. To fix this through co-design, you must implement continuous batching (iteration-level scheduling) to force the effective batch size higher (e.g., >32), amor
napkin_math: TPU v4 MXU is 128x128. A batch size of 1 means you are multiplying a 1xN vector by an NxM matrix. Only 1 row of the 128x128 array does useful work per cycle. Utilization = 1 / 128 = 0.78%. To hit >50% utilization, you need a batch size of at least 64.

title: MoE Routing Bandwidth Starvation
scenario: You deploy a 128-expert Mixture of Experts (MoE) transformer on a custom 8-NPU cloud node. Each NPU has 1 TB/s of HBM bandwidth. Standard top-2 token routing is used. Compute utilization is stuck at 12%. Profiling reveals the NPUs spend 85% of their time stalled on HBM reads, even though the dense compute equivalent runs at 70% utilization.
common_mistake: Attempting to increase the NPU clock speed or loop unrolling to process the compute faster.
realistic_solution: The problem requires hardware-algorithm co-design. Standard top-2 routing causes unstructured, all-to-all memory traffic. Because tokens can be routed to any of the 128 experts, the NPU must constantly page different expert weights in and out of its local SRAM from HBM. The tradeoff is model capacity utilization versus memory bandwidth efficiency. The solution is Expert Parallelism combined with routing constraints: pin 16 specific experts to each of the 8 NPUs and keep their weights stationary in SRAM/HBM. Modify the routing algorithm to penalize cross-NPU routing or enforce 'Expert Choice' r
napkin_math: 128 experts, each 1GB = 128GB total weights. If every NPU needs to load 128GB from HBM at 1 TB/s for a microbatch, memory load takes 128ms. If compute takes 15ms, utilization is 15 / (128 + 15) = 10.4%. Pinning experts reduces loads to 16GB per NPU (16ms), raising utilization to 15 / (16 + 15) = 48%.

title: FPGA Spatial Pipeline Mapping
scenario: A high-frequency trading firm needs a 5-layer MLP to execute in under 10 microseconds. On CPU, it takes 250us. You port the model to an AWS F1 FPGA using standard OpenCL High-Level Synthesis (HLS) with matrix multiplication kernels. The resulting FPGA latency is 180us, missing the SLA by 18x. DSP slice utilization is only 5%.
common_mistake: Increasing the batch size to improve throughput, completely ignoring the strict single-inference latency SLA.
realistic_solution: Standard HLS maps the MLP to a von Neumann-style execution: it builds a single Matrix Multiply unit and reuses it sequentially for each layer, loading weights from off-chip DRAM. The tradeoff here is chip area (DSP slices/BRAM) versus latency. To achieve <10us latency, you must co-design the algorithm mapping to use a fully unrolled spatial architecture. Because the model is small, you can store all weights entirely in distributed BRAM. You instantiate dedicated hardware ALUs for every single neuron in the 5 layers and pipeline them. Instead of sequential execution, the data flows through the
napkin_math: Sequential HLS: 5 layers * 1000 matrix ops * 5ns clock + DRAM access = ~180us. Spatial unroll: 5 layers * 5 pipeline stages per layer = 25 clock cycles. At 200MHz (5ns clock), latency = 25 * 5ns = 125 nanoseconds. This leaves ample headroom for PCIe/network overhead.

title: FP8 Quantization on Tensor Cores
scenario: Deploying a 70B parameter model on H100 GPUs using FP8. You use the E4M3 format (4 exponent bits, 3 mantissa) for both weights and activations. The model throughput increases by 1.8x, but accuracy drops by 22%. Profiling shows massive overflow errors in the activation tensors during attention computation.
common_mistake: Switching entirely back to FP16, losing the entire throughput advantage of the Hopper FP8 Tensor Cores.
realistic_solution: The issue is a mismatch between the numeric format and the statistical distribution of LLM activations. E4M3 has a very limited dynamic range (max value ~448). LLM activations notoriously contain massive outliers (values > 1000) that overflow E4M3. The tradeoff is precision (mantissa) versus dynamic range (exponent) mapped to hardware capabilities. Hopper Tensor Cores support mixed FP8 formats natively in a single MMA (Matrix Multiply-Accumulate) instruction. The co-design solution is to use E4M3 for the weights (which are normally distributed and bounded) and E5M2 (max value ~57344) for the a
napkin_math: E4M3 Max: 2^(15-7) * (1 - 2^-4) ≈ 448. E5M2 Max: 2^(31-15) * (1 - 2^-3) ≈ 57344. LLM activation outlier = 1200. In E4M3, 1200 is clipped to 448 (62% error). In E5M2, 1200 is represented with lower precision but no clipping, preserving the magnitude for the attention softmax.

title: Custom ASIC SRAM Tiling
scenario: You are writing the compiler for a new cloud NPU. The NPU has 16MB of ultra-fast SRAM (scratchpad) and 500GB/s HBM. Running self-attention on a sequence length of 4096 (hidden dim 1024, FP16) results in heavy HBM spilling. The attention block takes 45ms, severely underperforming the theoretical compute limit of 5ms.
common_mistake: Adding more HBM bandwidth to the chip design, which increases packaging costs and power consumption drastically.
realistic_solution: The latency is dominated by intermediate memory reads/writes to HBM because the full attention matrix does not fit in SRAM. Q, K, and V alone take up 24MB. The tradeoff is compute operations versus memory bandwidth. The hardware-compiler co-design solution is to implement FlashAttention-style tiling explicitly sized for the 16MB SRAM. By computing the softmax and attention weights in smaller blocks (e.g., 1024x1024 tiles), you keep the intermediate O(N^2) attention matrix entirely in SRAM. You trade an increase in FLOPs (recomputing the softmax denominator iteratively) for a massive reduction
napkin_math: Standard attention memory: Q, K, V are each 4096 * 1024 * 2 bytes = 8MB (Total 24MB). Attention matrix S = 4096 * 4096 * 2 bytes = 33MB. Total memory needed = 57MB. This heavily spills the 16MB SRAM. Tiling to 1024x1024 blocks requires only 1024*1024*2 = 2MB for S_tile, easily fitting in the 16MB SRAM and preventing 33MB of HBM roundtrips.

title: Chiplet Accelerator Interconnect Mapping
scenario: Evaluating a multi-die accelerator (4 chiplets on a single substrate) for a 175B model. Intra-chiplet bandwidth is 4 TB/s; inter-chiplet (UCIe) is 800 GB/s. Using standard Tensor Parallelism (TP) across the 4 chiplets yields only 25% scaling efficiency. The accelerator stalls during All-Reduce operations.
common_mistake: Assuming the UCIe interconnect is faulty and trying to compress the All-Reduce gradients using FP8 or INT8.
realistic_solution: The hardware architecture has a highly asymmetric bandwidth topology (4 TB/s inside, 800 GB/s outside). Tensor Parallelism requires massive, frequent All-Reduce communications of activations at every single transformer layer. The tradeoff is communication frequency versus memory distribution. The hardware-algorithm co-design solution is to map Pipeline Parallelism (PP) across the chiplets instead of TP. PP only passes the final layer activations forward to the next chiplet at stage boundaries, drastically reducing the communication volume to fit within the 800 GB/s inter-chiplet bottleneck, wh
napkin_math: For a sequence of 2048, hidden dim 12288, FP16: Activation size = 2048 * 12288 * 2 = 50MB. TP All-Reduce across 4 chips per layer (96 layers) at 800 GB/s takes ~0.2ms per layer * 96 = 19.2ms. PP only sends 50MB once per microbatch across the chiplet boundary, taking 50MB / 800GB/s = 0.06ms of communication time.

title: Unstructured vs Structured Sparsity
scenario: You prune a ResNet-50 model to 60% unstructured sparsity to reduce inference costs. You deploy it on an A100 GPU using cuSPARSE. Profiling shows absolutely 0% improvement in latency; both the dense and sparse models take 12ms per batch of 32. Total FLOPs processed by the hardware remain identical.
common_mistake: Increasing the sparsity level to 80%, hoping the zero-skipping finally kicks in on the GPU.
realistic_solution: Standard GPUs and their Tensor Cores compute dense matrix blocks. Unstructured sparsity means the zeros are randomly distributed. The GPU must still load the full dense memory blocks from HBM and execute the dense MMA instructions; multiplying by zero takes the same time as multiplying by one. The tradeoff is pruning flexibility versus hardware mapping. To achieve acceleration, you must co-design the pruning algorithm with the Ampere architecture. A100 features Sparse Tensor Cores that explicitly support 2:4 structured sparsity (exactly 2 zeros in every block of 4 elements). By retraining the
napkin_math: Unstructured 60% sparsity: Hardware processes 100% of the dense matrix. Throughput = 1x. Structured 2:4 sparsity (50% sparse): The Sparse Tensor Core compresses the matrix by 50% in memory and skips the zeros in the ALU. Throughput = 2x. You trade 10% theoretical sparsity for 100% actual hardware acceleration.

title: Arithmetic Intensity & Cache Thrashing
scenario: Serving two models on the same A100 GPU via MPS (Multi-Process Service): a memory-bound sparse embedding lookup model (500 QPS) and a compute-bound dense MLP (100 QPS). When run in isolation, MLP latency is 15ms. When co-located, MLP latency spikes to 85ms. GPU compute utilization remains low.
common_mistake: Using CUDA streams to enforce sequential execution, which destroys the throughput benefits of co-location.
realistic_solution: This is a hardware-system co-design problem driven by arithmetic intensity. MPS shares the GPU spatially but leaves the L2 cache (40MB on A100) shared globally. The memory-bound embedding model constantly streams massive amounts of data from HBM, completely evicting the L2 cache. The compute-bound MLP relies heavily on L2 cache hits for weight reuse. When co-located, the embedding model thrashes the cache, forcing the MLP to fetch from slow HBM. The tradeoff is dynamic resource sharing (MPS) versus strict isolation (MIG). The solution is to switch to Multi-Instance GPU (MIG), which physically
napkin_math: Embedding model streams 40 GB/s. The 40MB L2 cache is entirely overwritten every 1 millisecond (40MB / 40GB/s). The MLP execution takes 15ms, meaning its weights are evicted 15 times per inference. Fetching from HBM (1.5TB/s) instead of L2 (5TB/s) incurs a 3.3x bandwidth penalty, causing the 5x latency spike due to memory stalls.

title: Serving 70B LLMs on Single Node
scenario: You need to deploy a 70B parameter LLM for a global chatbot service. The hardware budget strictly limits you to a single node with 4x A10G (24GB VRAM each, 96GB total). The throughput requirement is 500 tokens/sec. The model in FP16 requires ~140GB just for weights. How do you architect the deployment to meet memory constraints while maximizing generation throughput?
common_mistake: Applying W8A8 quantization to reduce weights and compute, which degrades accuracy and doesn't maximize generation token throughput since generation is memory-bandwidth bound.
realistic_solution: Implement Weight-Only Post-Training Quantization (e.g., AWQ or GPTQ) down to 4-bit (INT4). In autoregressive generation, the process is heavily memory-bandwidth bound (batch size of 1 per step implies reading the entire weight matrix to generate a single token). By quantizing weights to INT4 and keeping activations in FP16 (W4A16), you reduce the memory footprint to fit within the 96GB limit with plenty of room for the KV cache, while drastically reducing the bytes read from VRAM per token, directly accelerating generation throughput. A calibration set must be used during the PTQ process to pr
napkin_math: FP16 weights: 70B * 2 bytes = 140GB (OOM on 96GB node). INT4 weights: 70B * 0.5 bytes = 35GB. VRAM remaining: 96GB - 35GB = 61GB. KV Cache per token (Llama-2 70B GQA): 80 layers * 8 KV heads * 128 dim * 2 (K+V) * 2 bytes = 327KB/token. 61GB allows for ~186,000 tokens in the KV cache, easily supporting high concurrency.

title: Hopper FP8 Format Mismatch
scenario: Migrating a dense 100B transformer to Hopper H100 GPUs, the infrastructure team switches the serving engine to use FP8 to double the MAC throughput (target: 8000 QPS). They blindly cast all FP16 weights and activations to the E5M2 FP8 format. Benchmarks show the target throughput is reached, but the model's accuracy drops catastrophically (MMLU score drops from 72% to 45%).
common_mistake: Assuming all FP8 formats are interchangeable and applying E5M2 uniformly to maximize dynamic range, destroying the mantissa precision needed for forward-pass inference.
realistic_solution: The architecture must selectively utilize the two distinct FP8 formats: E4M3 (4 exponent bits, 3 mantissa bits) and E5M2 (5 exponent bits, 2 mantissa bits). E5M2 provides a larger dynamic range (like FP16) which is highly beneficial for gradient representation during training. However, for inference (weights and forward activations), dynamic range is less critical than precision. The solution is to cast weights and activations to E4M3, which provides an extra bit of mantissa (higher precision), effectively preserving the model's accuracy. Furthermore, per-tensor or per-channel scaling factors
napkin_math: E5M2 has 2 mantissa bits: 2^2 = 4 fractional values per bin, max value ~57344. E4M3 has 3 mantissa bits: 2^3 = 8 fractional values per bin (double the precision), max value ~448. Inference activations rarely exceed 448 (especially with LayerNorm), making E4M3 the mathematically superior choice for reducing quantization noise in the forward pass.

title: Long-Context VRAM Exhaustion
scenario: You are serving a 34B parameter code generation model with a massive 64k token context window on 2x A100 (80GB) GPUs. The model weights are quantized to INT4 (occupying ~17GB). At a concurrency of just 20 requests, the system hits Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors and crashes, even though compute utilization is below 40%.
common_mistake: Aggressively quantizing the model weights further to INT3 or INT2, which degrades accuracy but fails to solve the OOM because the KV cache is the actual memory bottleneck.
realistic_solution: The architectural requirement is to implement KV Cache Quantization. While weights are INT4, the KV cache (storing past keys and values for attention) is likely defaulting to FP16. At long context lengths, the KV cache footprint grows linearly and surpasses the weight footprint. By quantizing the KV cache dynamically to INT8 (or FP8 on supported hardware) using techniques like per-token or per-channel asymmetric quantization, you halve the memory required per token. This allows the system to support the required 64k context at 20+ concurrency without OOMing, while maintaining long-context retr
napkin_math: Assume 60 layers, 64 heads, 128 head dim. FP16 KV cache per token: 60 * 64 * 128 * 2 (K+V) * 2 bytes = ~1.96 MB/token. For 64k context: 1.96 MB * 65536 = 128 GB per request! Across 20 requests = 2.56 TB (impossible on 160GB total VRAM). Using Grouped Query Attention (GQA, e.g. 8 KV heads) reduces this to 16 GB/req. Still, 20 reqs = 320GB. Quantizing KV to INT8 (1 byte) halves it to 160GB, and using INT4 KV cache (0.5 bytes) brings it to 80GB, fitting comfortably within the 160GB VRAM budget.
